proposals.title: First-Principles, Spatially Resolved Dielectric Maps from Local Electronic Structure for Predictive Macromolecular Electrostatics
proposals.motivation: Accurate modeling of electrostatic interactions is critical for understanding protein function, ligand binding, and catalysis, yet current continuum solvation models rely on a simplistic, homogeneous dielectric constant for the protein interior. This parameter is often fitted to experiment, lacks physical rigor, and fails to capture the heterogeneous, anisotropic electronic polarizability inherent in a folded protein's structure. A first-principles, spatially resolved dielectric model would provide a more predictive foundation for computational biophysics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a spatially varying, position-dependent dielectric constant for a macromolecule can be rigorously derived from its local electronic structure, and that this first-principles dielectric map will yield more accurate and transferable predictions of electrostatic energies and pKa shifts than current uniform or distance-dependent dielectric models.
proposals.method: We will develop a workflow using the ONETEP linear-scaling Density Functional Theory (DFT) code to compute the electron density and polarizability tensor for entire protein structures. A constitutive model will be built to translate local quantum mechanical descriptors (e.g., electron density, localized polarizability) into a scalar or tensor dielectric value at each point in space. This dielectric map will be integrated into the Amber/MMPBSA framework via a modified Poisson-Boltzmann solver to compute electrostatic energies. Validation will involve calculating pKa values for buried residues in a test set of enzymes and comparing to experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, parameter-free method for defining the protein interior dielectric, directly linking continuum electrostatics to quantum mechanical reality. It will provide the community with a robust computational tool for more accurate prediction of binding affinities and protonation states, advancing studies of enzyme mechanism and molecular recognition. The approach bridges quantum chemistry and classical biomolecular simulation in a physically grounded way.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have suggested heterogeneous dielectric models, none have derived the spatial variation directly and rigorously from the protein's own computed electronic structure, eliminating empirical parameterization. This moves beyond fitting dielectric constants to properties, instead predicting them from first principles.

proposals.title: Anisotropic Voronoi Tessellation of Atomic Volumes at Grain Boundaries: A Method Derived from Protein Surface Analysis
proposals.motivation: In materials science, accurately modeling atomic volumes at grain boundaries (GBs) is critical for predicting segregation, diffusion, and mechanical properties. Current models often assume spherical or static ellipsoidal atomic volumes, which fail to capture the complex, anisotropic atomic environments present at interfaces. This limitation hinders the accurate calculation of fundamental thermodynamic quantities like excess volume and chemical potential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that atomic volumes at grain boundaries can be more accurately and fundamentally described by an anisotropic Voronoi tessellation, where the partitioning metric is derived from local atomic properties, analogous to how solvent-accessible surfaces are computed for proteins.
proposals.method: We will adapt the concept of the rolling probe algorithm (e.g., Shrake-Rupley method) from protein surface analysis to define a non-Euclidean distance metric for Voronoi tessellation. For each atom, a local anisotropy tensor will be constructed from pre-2026 data, such as atomic displacement parameters from crystallography or elastic tensors from DFT calculations. The space in the GB region will be partitioned by assigning every point to the atom for which the anisotropic distance, defined as d = sqrt[(r_ij)^T M_i (r_ij)], is minimized, where M_i is the atom-specific metric tensor. This will be implemented within existing molecular simulation and analysis frameworks (e.g., LAMMPS, OVITO) to process atomic models of GBs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, physically grounded computational tool for defining atomic volumes in non-uniform environments, directly linking local atomic properties to geometric partitioning. It will enable more accurate calculations of excess properties at interfaces and offer a novel perspective for analyzing atomic-scale free volume and packing, with implications for understanding segregation and diffusion kinetics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in translating a well-established algorithm from computational biology (solvent-accessible surface calculation) to a fundamental problem in materials science interface modeling, replacing static shape assumptions with a dynamic, property-driven tessellation. This moves beyond simple geometric constructs to a physics-based definition of atomic volume.

proposals.title: Parameterizing Solvent Response: A Generalized Born Model Optimized for Nucleic Acid Electrostatics and Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Generalized Born (GB) models are essential for efficient implicit-solvent molecular dynamics simulations of biomolecules, but their standard parameterizations are primarily optimized for proteins. Nucleic acids present a distinct electrostatic challenge due to their dense, highly charged phosphate backbone and base stacking, leading to known inaccuracies in modeling salt-dependent stability, ion binding, and conformational dynamics. Improving GB accuracy for nucleic acids is critical for reliable computational studies of RNA folding, protein-DNA recognition, and nucleic acid-based therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a GB model with parameters specifically derived from explicit-solvent reference calculations on a diverse set of nucleic acid conformations and sequences will yield significantly improved accuracy in predicting electrostatic solvation energies, ion atmosphere effects, and correlated dynamics compared to existing protein-centric models.
proposals.method: We will employ the robust total X-ray scattering analysis framework from the researcher's recent work to inform on correlated motions in nucleic acid crystal structures as a validation target. The parameter optimization will use a large training set of DNA and RNA structures (from B-DNA to complex folds like riboswitches) to compute reference Poisson-Boltzmann (PB) electrostatic solvation energies. We will then systematically optimize the key GB parameters (e.g., intrinsic radii, scaling factors) in the Amber/ff99SB+OL3 force field using a least-squares fitting algorithm against the PB reference data. The optimized model will be validated by its ability to reproduce experimental observables such as small-angle X-ray scattering profiles, NMR order parameters, and binding free energies for protein-nucleic acid complexes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, optimized GB parameter set for nucleic acids within the widely used Amber simulation package, directly enabling more accurate and efficient simulations of nucleic acid structure, dynamics, and binding. It will provide a foundational improvement for studying biologically crucial processes like transcription factor binding and RNA catalysis with implicit solvent, bridging the gap between detailed explicit-solvent simulations and high-throughput applications.
proposals.novelty: While previous GB optimizations have focused on proteins or overall benchmarks, this proposal uniquely centers on the distinct physics of the nucleic acid electrostatic environment, leveraging the researcher's expertise in correlated motion analysis to create a model validated for dynamic ensembles, not just static structures.

proposals.title: Analytical Generalized Born Gradients for Enhanced Sampling of Protein Dynamics and Solvent Interactions
proposals.motivation: Generalized Born (GB) implicit solvent models are essential for efficient molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of proteins, enabling longer timescales and improved conformational sampling. However, the computational cost of calculating forces (energy gradients) for pair-wise GB models remains high, as many implementations rely on numerical or semi-analytical approximations. This bottleneck limits the ability to perform robust, long-timescale simulations needed to study correlated motions, allosteric regulation, and binding events, which are central themes in Dr. Case's research on protein dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deriving and implementing fully analytical energy gradients for pair-wise GB models will significantly improve the computational efficiency and numerical stability of MD simulations, enabling more accurate and extensive sampling of protein conformational dynamics and solvent-mediated interactions.
proposals.method: We will derive the complete analytical first derivatives (gradients) for the pairwise GB model, specifically focusing on the Still et al. and related formulations, by applying the chain rule to all terms, including the effective Born radii and their spatial dependencies. The derived equations will be implemented and optimized within the Amber and/or CHARMM simulation packages, leveraging their existing MD architectures. The implementation's accuracy will be validated against finite-difference gradients, and its efficiency will be benchmarked on model systems (e.g., phosphatidylinositol-binding proteins) and larger enzymes to quantify speed-up in production dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a robust, efficient, and open-source implementation of analytical GB gradients, directly accelerating MD simulations of biomolecular dynamics. It will enable longer and more statistically rigorous simulations to study correlated motions and binding mechanisms, bridging the gap between computational efficiency and the detailed sampling required to interpret experimental data from X-ray scattering and NMR.
proposals.novelty: While GB models are widely used, a fully general and widely adopted analytical gradient formulation for pairwise GB remains non-trivial and not universally implemented; this project systematically derives and disseminates this critical component, moving beyond the numerical approximations that limit current performance.

proposals.title: A Dynamically Adaptive QM/MM-GB Method with a Blended Transition Zone for Simulating Reactive Events in Proteins
proposals.motivation: Hybrid QM/MM simulations are essential for studying enzyme catalysis and ligand binding, but their accuracy is compromised by a static QM region boundary that cannot adapt to evolving electronic structure during a reaction. Furthermore, coupling a QM region to a Generalized Born (GB) continuum solvent model introduces artifacts at the interface, especially when charged or polar species move. A method that dynamically redefines the QM core while ensuring smooth, physically consistent coupling to the MM-GB environment is a critical, unsolved challenge in computational biophysics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid QM/MM-GB method featuring a dynamically adaptive QM region, managed by an algorithm monitoring local electronic and structural changes, and connected to the MM-GB continuum via a mathematically smoothed transition layer, will significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of simulating reactive events in proteins.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithm that monitors atomic forces, partial charges, or bond-order changes in the MM region to trigger the adaptive inclusion/exclusion of residues into the QM core. Inspired by GIS terrain-blending techniques, we will implement a smoothed transition layer using a differentiable switching function to modulate Hamiltonian terms between the QM and MM-GB regions. This function will ensure energy conservation and smooth force transitions. The method will be integrated into the Amber/PMEMD simulation suite, leveraging its existing QM/MM and GB (GBSA-OBC/GB-neck2) frameworks, and validated on benchmark systems like phosphoryl transfer in a kinase.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, generalizable framework for adaptive QM/MM simulations with implicit solvent, directly addressing the problem of boundary errors in reactive processes. It will enable more accurate and computationally tractable studies of enzymatic mechanisms and ligand-binding dynamics where the reactive center evolves, a key interest in Dr. Case's research on correlated protein motions and binding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual translation of adaptive mesh refinement and blending from GIS to the QM/MM boundary problem, and in the specific algorithmic implementation for dynamically managing the QM region within a GB continuum, which goes beyond existing static or manually adjusted schemes.

proposals.title: Mapping Enzymatic Correlated Motions with QM/MM-GBSA: A Hybrid Simulation Framework for Phosphatidylinositol Recognition
proposals.motivation: Understanding the role of correlated protein motions in enzymatic function and ligand binding, such as in phosphatidylinositol recognition, requires accurate modeling of both electronic structure effects and long-range solvent interactions. Traditional molecular mechanics (MM) force fields lack the quantum mechanical (QM) detail needed for processes like charge transfer or polarization, while explicit-solvent QM/MM simulations remain computationally prohibitive for studying the long-timescale dynamics observed in recent X-ray scattering experiments. A robust, efficient hybrid method is needed to bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combining a QM region for the active site with a Generalized Born (GB) solvated MM environment will enable efficient, accurate simulations of correlated protein motions, capturing the essential physics of ligand binding and enzymatic catalysis while maintaining computational tractability for dynamic analysis.
proposals.method: We will implement a QM/MM-GBSA framework using the Amber/NAMD simulation packages. The QM region (e.g., phosphatidylinositol headgroup and key protein residues) will be treated with DFT (e.g., B3LYP) or semi-empirical methods (e.g., SCC-DFTB) interfaced via an electrostatic embedding scheme. The MM protein environment will use the ff14SB force field, with solvent effects modeled by the GB-OBC (Onufriev-Bashford-Case) implicit solvent model. We will employ adaptive sampling and correlation analysis of trajectories to quantify long-range motional couplings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated computational workflow for studying correlated protein dynamics with quantum mechanical accuracy, directly linking to experimental X-ray scattering data. It will yield new mechanistic insights into how solvent-mediated electrostatic interactions modulate collective motions during phosphatidylinositol binding and enzymatic action.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic integration of a well-validated GB solvent model within a QM/MM framework specifically tailored for analyzing long-timescale correlated motions, moving beyond static energy calculations to dynamic, solvent-aware quantum-biophysical simulations.

proposals.title: Scalable Generalized Born Models for Microsecond Dynamics of Supramolecular Assemblies
proposals.motivation: Understanding the functional dynamics of massive biomolecular complexes like viral capsids and ribosomes is crucial for elucidating mechanisms of infection and translation. All-atom explicit solvent molecular dynamics (MD) of these systems is computationally prohibitive beyond short timescales, limiting insight into large-scale, functionally relevant motions. Generalized Born (GB) implicit solvent models offer a 10-100x speedup but have not been robustly scaled and validated for systems exceeding several million atoms due to algorithmic bottlenecks and parallelization challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: By developing a massively parallel, domain-decomposed Generalized Born framework integrated with modern MD codes, we can achieve microsecond-timescale simulations of entire supramolecular assemblies, revealing correlated motions and solvation dynamics inaccessible to current methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the GBNeck2 or GBSW implicit solvent model within the Amber or OpenMM MD engines. Key innovations will include: 1) A hierarchical, spatial-decomposition algorithm for the O(N^2) pairwise Born radii calculation, leveraging MPI for inter-node communication and GPU acceleration for intra-node computations. 2) Integration of a multi-scale 'solvent shell' model to retain explicit solvent in critical binding pockets (e.g., active sites), addressing a known limitation of pure GB for ligand binding. 3) Validation against existing total X-ray scattering data and explicit solvent MD benchmarks for ribosomal subunits and viral capsid fragments to ensure physical accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source, production-ready software module enabling the simulation of billion-atom biological systems at microsecond timescales on modern supercomputers. It will provide unprecedented atomic-level insight into the collective dynamics governing the function of ribosomes and viral capsids, directly testing hypotheses about long-range communication and allostery.
proposals.novelty: While GB models are well-established for proteins, their application to multi-million atom complexes requires a fundamental re-engineering of the computational workflow for massive parallelism, which has not been achieved. The hybrid explicit/implicit solvation strategy tailored for massive systems is also a novel integration to address accuracy concerns at binding interfaces.

proposals.title: A Linear-Scaling Semiempirical QM/MM-GB Method for High-Throughput, Quantum-Aware Scoring in Virtual Screening
proposals.motivation: Current virtual screening relies heavily on empirical scoring functions, which lack explicit quantum mechanical (QM) treatment of critical interactions like charge transfer, polarization, and halogen bonding, limiting accuracy. While full QM/MM methods are accurate, they are computationally prohibitive for screening millions of compounds. There is a critical need for a scoring method that incorporates essential QM physics at a cost compatible with high-throughput pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by coupling a minimal, linear-scaling semiempirical QM method (e.g., DFTB or NDDO-based) for the ligand with a pre-computed, ultra-fast Generalized Born (GB) reaction field for the protein and solvent, we can achieve quantum-aware binding energy estimates at speeds suitable for large-scale virtual screening.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid method where the ligand or its key pharmacophore is treated with the GFN-xTB or PM6-D3H4 semiempirical Hamiltonian. The protein's electrostatic influence will be captured via a pre-computed GB reaction field grid, derived from a single GB calculation on the apo protein structure, parameterized using the GBNSR6 model for its speed and accuracy. A key innovation will be a simplified coupling scheme where the QM region's electrostatic potential directly polarizes this static GB field, avoiding iterative GB solves. The workflow will be integrated into the Amber/OpenMM ecosystem for pose generation and MM minimization prior to the final QM/MM-GB scoring step.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first genuinely high-throughput QM/MM scoring pipeline, enabling the quantum-aware screening of massive compound libraries. It will provide a concrete computational tool to study how electronic structure details influence binding selectivity, directly complementing Dr. Case's research on correlated protein motions and binding. The method will be validated on benchmark sets focusing on interactions problematic for MM, such as metalloenzymes and halogen bonds.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the individual QM or GB components, but in their fusion into a non-iterative, grid-based coupling scheme that drastically reduces cost while retaining a quantum-mechanical description of the ligand, moving beyond purely classical or QM-clustering approaches to screening.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Explicit/Implicit Ion QM/MM-GB Method for Modeling Ion-Specific Modulation of Enzyme Dynamics and Reactivity
proposals.motivation: Accurate modeling of electrostatic environments in biomolecular simulations is critical for understanding enzyme catalysis and ligand binding, where specific ion identity and local concentration can dramatically alter reaction barriers and affinities. Standard QM/MM methods with pure continuum solvent (e.g., Generalized Born) fail to capture these specific ion effects, while fully explicit solvent simulations with ions are computationally prohibitive for QM-driven dynamics. A balanced, high-fidelity electrostatic model is needed to connect molecular motions to chemical function in ion-rich environments like metalloenzyme active sites.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding a discrete, mobile shell of explicit ions and counterions within the GB continuum around a QM region will yield a computationally tractable model that accurately captures how specific ions modulate local electric fields, electronic polarization, and correlated protein motions to influence reactivity.
proposals.method: We will develop and implement a hybrid QM/MM-GB model where a defined region around the QM subsystem contains explicit, mobile ions modeled with MM force fields. These ions will interact with the QM electron density via electrostatic embedding and with the MM protein atoms, while their long-range solvation and interaction with the bulk will be governed by a modified GB framework that accounts for their discrete presence. The method will be integrated into the Amber/PMEMD simulation suite, leveraging its existing QM/MM and GB capabilities. We will validate the approach by calculating pKa shifts in ion-binding sites and reaction profiles for model phosphoryl transfer reactions in the presence of different alkali and alkaline earth metal ions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the computational biophysics community with a practical, high-accuracy tool for studying ion-coupled reactivity and dynamics, directly complementing Dr. Case's recent work on correlated protein motions. It will yield new mechanistic insights into how specific ions, not just ionic strength, fine-tune enzyme function and spectroscopic properties, bridging the gap between static structure and dynamic function.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, dynamic coupling of discrete ions to a QM region within a GB continuum, moving beyond static ion placements or mean-field approximations to create a uniquely responsive electrostatic environment for modeling reactivity.

proposals.title: Mapping Ligand-Induced Perturbations to the Energy Landscape and Spectral Diffusion Dynamics of Myoglobin via Total X-ray Scattering and Molecular Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Proteins are inherently dynamic, and their functional energy landscapes are shaped by motions across multiple timescales. Myoglobin (Mb) serves as a paradigm for studying ligand binding, but a quantitative, atomic-level understanding of how small diatomic ligands (CO vs. O2) differentially alter the protein's conformational landscape and the associated spectral diffusion observed in time-resolved spectroscopy remains incomplete. This gap limits our ability to connect specific ligand-protein interactions to the modulation of functional dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CO and O2 binding to myoglobin will induce distinct, quantifiable changes in the correlated atomic motions and the roughness of the underlying energy landscape, which directly manifest as differences in spectral diffusion dynamics measured by ultrafast spectroscopy.
proposals.method: We will employ a robust total X-ray scattering (TXS) workflow on high-quality Mb crystals in the unliganded, CO-bound, and O2-bound states to experimentally capture correlated atomic displacements. These experimental datasets will be integrated with molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using the Amber force field, explicitly including solvent and, where relevant, phosphatidylinositol membrane-mimetic environments. The MD simulations will be analyzed using principal component analysis (PCA) and time-lagged independent component analysis (tICA) to characterize changes in the collective motion landscape. Spectral diffusion will be modeled by calculating time-dependent fluctuations of the heme pocket environment from the simulation trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, experimentally validated link between ligand-specific structural perturbations and the modulation of myoglobin's energy landscape. It will deliver a mechanistic explanation for differences in spectral diffusion signals between CO and O2 photoproducts, bridging structural biology and ultrafast spectroscopy. The integrated TXS/MD methodology will be a generalizable framework for studying ligand effects on protein dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of total X-ray scattering data—sensitive to correlated motions in the crystal—with advanced landscape analysis of MD simulations to decode ligand-specific effects, moving beyond static structures or simulation-only studies to a truly multi-method investigation of landscape perturbation.

proposals.title: Mapping Spectral Diffusion to Atomic Motions: An All-Atom Molecular Dynamics Study of Enzyme Conformational Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Recent experimental advances, such as 2D infrared spectroscopy and total X-ray scattering, have revealed pervasive, correlated motions in proteins that are crucial for function, including ligand binding and catalysis. However, interpreting the spectral diffusion steps observed in these experiments in terms of specific, atomic-level conformational transitions remains a significant challenge. This gap limits our ability to validate and refine the proposed energy landscapes that govern protein dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that long-timescale, all-atom molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, when integrated with spectral density calculations, can directly map observed spectral diffusion events to specific, collective atomic motions, thereby providing a rigorous, atomistic validation of the protein's conformational energy landscape.
proposals.method: We will perform microsecond-scale, all-atom MD simulations using the AMBER force field (ff19SB) and the TIP4P-Ew water model for a well-studied enzymatic system, such as cyclophilin A, known for its conformational dynamics. Simulations will be run on GPU-accelerated hardware using the pmemd.cuda engine. Time-dependent fluctuations of electric fields at key spectroscopic probes (e.g., carbonyl groups) will be calculated from the trajectories. These will be used to compute spectral densities and model spectral diffusion, which will be directly compared to experimental 2D-IR or NMR relaxation dispersion data. Markov State Models (MSMs) will be constructed to identify the metastable states and transition pathways underlying the observed spectral dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, atomic-resolution interpretation of spectral diffusion data, bridging the gap between ensemble-averaged spectroscopic observables and discrete molecular motions. It will deliver a validated, quantitative model of the conformational landscape for a model enzyme, demonstrating a generalizable workflow for connecting dynamics to function. The results will strengthen the theoretical foundation for understanding how correlated motions facilitate processes like substrate binding and catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While MD is often used to interpret dynamics, the direct, quantitative connection of simulated atomic trajectories to experimental spectral diffusion steps via spectral density calculations is not routine. This approach moves beyond simple correlation of timescales to a mechanistic assignment of the specific atomic rearrangements responsible for each observed spectroscopic feature.

proposals.title: Deconvolving the Energetic Landscape of Multi-Domain Proteins: A Combined NMR, X-ray Scattering, and MD Study of Isolated and Tethered Domains
proposals.motivation: Multi-domain proteins often exhibit complex, correlated motions essential for function, such as allosteric regulation and ligand binding. However, in full-length proteins, the contributions of individual domains to the collective conformational landscape are convoluted and difficult to parse. A systematic, domain-by-domain experimental and computational analysis is needed to understand how intrinsic domain dynamics are modulated by inter-domain tethering and interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: The conformational landscape and dynamics of a multi-domain protein can be quantitatively reconstructed from the intrinsic properties of its isolated domains, plus the energetic and dynamic perturbations introduced by their covalent linkage and interfacial contacts.
proposals.method: We will select a model multi-domain protein (e.g., a signaling protein with SH3, SH2, and kinase domains). Isolated domains will be expressed and studied using solution NMR (relaxation, residual dipolar couplings) to characterize intrinsic dynamics and conformational ensembles. Total X-ray scattering (Solution SAXS/WAXS) will provide pair distribution functions for domain shapes. These experimental data will be used to refine and validate molecular dynamics simulations (using AMBER with explicit solvent and enhanced sampling techniques like replica exchange) of both isolated domains and the full-length protein. The MD-derived ensembles will be analyzed using correlation matrices and principal component analysis to map domain-specific motions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative framework for dissecting the origins of correlated motion in proteins. It will yield a foundational understanding of how domain tethering sculpts the free energy landscape, directly informing models of allostery and molecular recognition. The integrated experimental-computational workflow will serve as a blueprint for studying other modular protein systems.
proposals.novelty: While domain studies are common, this proposal uniquely combines high-resolution NMR dynamics, total X-ray scattering of correlated motion, and ensemble-refined MD in a single, systematic pipeline to explicitly deconvolve and then reconstitute the full protein landscape from its parts, moving beyond qualitative descriptions to a predictive, energy-based model.

proposals.title: Mapping the Conformational Landscape of Chemically Denatured Myoglobin Using Total X-ray Scattering and Molecular Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Understanding the conformational ensembles of denatured proteins is crucial for elucidating protein folding mechanisms and the nature of the unfolded state, which remains poorly characterized compared to native structures. Pre-2026, David Case's work has advanced the study of correlated motions in native proteins, but applying these robust experimental and computational techniques to the denatured state presents a significant opportunity. This study addresses a fundamental gap in structural biology by moving beyond static, native-centric views to probe the dynamic energy landscape of a protein under denaturing conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chemically denatured myoglobin samples a broad but non-random conformational landscape, characterized by transient, residual structural preferences and correlated motions that can be captured by integrating total X-ray scattering with explicit-solvent molecular dynamics simulations.
proposals.method: We will apply the robust total X-ray scattering (TXS) workflow developed by Case's group for studying correlated motions in protein crystals to solutions of horse heart myoglobin denatured in 6 M guanidinium chloride. The experimental scattering data, sensitive to ensemble-averaged pair-distance distributions, will be directly compared to scattering profiles computed from extensive, explicit-solvent molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using the Amber force field. The MD simulations will be initiated from the unfolded state and analyzed using principal component analysis and time-lagged independent component analysis to map the dominant conformational modes and free energy basins.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution, experimentally-validated map of the conformational free energy landscape for a chemically denatured globular protein. It will establish a novel TXS/MD integration framework for studying non-native ensembles, offering critical insights into residual structure, chain compaction, and the nature of dynamics in the unfolded state. The findings will directly inform models of protein folding and misfolding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of a cutting-edge, correlation-sensitive structural biology technique—total X-ray scattering from crystals—to the historically difficult-to-study denatured state in solution, coupled with rigorous validation against explicit-solvent MD simulations. This moves beyond simple radius of gyration measurements to probe the detailed shape and dynamics of the unfolded ensemble.

proposals.title: Integrating Correlated Motions and Electrostatic Complementarity for the Computational Design of High-Affinity Ligands
proposals.motivation: Computational ligand design often treats protein targets as rigid scaffolds, ignoring the dynamic nature of binding sites and the critical role of long-range electrostatic steering. Pre-2026 work, including Dr. Case's research on pervasive protein dynamics and correlated motions, demonstrates that conformational flexibility and electrostatics are fundamental to molecular recognition and binding kinetics. A design framework that explicitly incorporates these elements is needed to move beyond static, shape-centric models and improve the success rate of designed binders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constraining computational ligand design using both the dynamic ensemble of protein conformations and the long-range electrostatic potential of the target will yield ligands with superior binding affinity and specificity compared to designs based on a single static structure.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-stage computational pipeline. First, we will generate an ensemble of protein conformations using molecular dynamics (MD) simulations informed by experimental data (e.g., from total X-ray scattering). Second, we will use the APBS software to calculate the time-averaged electrostatic potential map for this ensemble. Third, we will integrate this dynamic electrostatic field as a spatial constraint into the Rosetta design framework, guiding ligand docking and side-chain optimization to achieve electrostatic complementarity across the relevant conformational states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, publicly available design protocol that unifies protein dynamics and electrostatics for ligand optimization. It will yield testable predictions for high-affinity binders to a model system (e.g., a phosphorylated phosphatidylinositol-binding domain), advancing our fundamental understanding of the interplay between motion, electrostatics, and binding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of a dynamic, ensemble-derived electrostatic potential—rather than a static field—as a primary constraint in a mainstream protein design software, explicitly linking Dr. Case's expertise in correlated motions with the optimization of binding interactions.

proposals.title: Mapping the Time-Dependent Electrostatic Landscape: A High-Throughput Analysis of Correlated Motions in Enzymatic Catalysis
proposals.motivation: Electrostatic interactions are fundamental to enzyme catalysis, ligand binding, and allostery, but are typically analyzed using static structures or averaged properties from simulations. This fails to capture how rapid, correlated protein motions dynamically modulate the electrostatic environment. Understanding these time-resolved electrostatic fluctuations is crucial for linking protein dynamics to function, a central theme in Dr. Case's recent work on pervasive dynamics and correlated motions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rapid, successive analysis of electrostatic potentials along molecular dynamics trajectories will reveal transient, correlated electrostatic states that are critical for enzymatic function but are obscured in static or time-averaged analyses.
proposals.method: We will develop an automated workflow using the APBS (Adaptive Poisson-Boltzmann Solver) software integrated with the AmberTools suite. The method will compute electrostatic potentials (including solvent and ion effects via the Poisson-Boltzmann equation) for thousands of successive snapshots from explicit-solvent MD trajectories of model enzymes (e.g., dihydrofolate reductase). Key innovations include GPU-accelerated APBS calculations and a novel correlation analysis to identify concerted changes in electrostatic potential across the protein structure over time, linking them to specific conformational transitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new computational toolset for 'electrostatic trajectory' analysis, directly connecting the correlated motions observed in Dr. Case's X-ray scattering studies to their functional electrostatic consequences. It will yield mechanistic insights into how enzymes utilize dynamics to pre-organize electrostatic environments for catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While electrostatic calculations on MD snapshots exist, the novelty lies in the scale, speed, and specific focus on identifying *correlated* electrostatic fluctuations over time as a direct probe of functionally important dynamics, moving beyond static pictures or simple averages.

proposals.title: A Coarse-Grained Normal Mode Analysis Framework for Efficiently Modeling Correlated Motions in Large Biomolecular Assemblies
proposals.motivation: Understanding the functional dynamics of large biomolecular complexes, such as ribosomes or viral capsids, is crucial for deciphering mechanisms in signaling and catalysis. Current molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are computationally prohibitive for these systems on biologically relevant timescales, while traditional normal mode analysis (NMA) struggles with the memory and efficiency demands of systems with hundreds of thousands of atoms. There is a pressing need for robust, scalable algorithms to model the collective, correlated motions that govern assembly function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid coarse-graining approach, combining an elastic network model with a targeted all-atom representation of key interfacial residues, can accurately capture the dominant low-frequency, correlated motions of mega-Dalton complexes with near-linear computational scaling.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-tiered NMA workflow. First, we will implement a Cα-based elastic network model using a block Hessian formulation and iterative Lanczos diagonalization to efficiently extract the lowest-frequency modes. Second, we will integrate a local all-atom NMA for pre-defined binding sites or catalytic centers, informed by Dr. Case's expertise in solvent and phosphatidylinositol interactions. The coupling between coarse and fine regions will be handled via a projection and back-mapping scheme. Algorithm robustness will be validated against total X-ray scattering data and experimental B-factors from crystals of known large complexes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source software tool that enables the efficient analysis of correlated dynamics in structures beyond the current practical limit of ~500,000 atoms. It will provide a direct computational link to experimental measures of motion in crystals, advancing the integration of simulation and scattering data. The method will offer new insights into how large-scale conformational changes emerge from local interactions in assemblies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific hybrid coarse-graining strategy that maintains atomic detail at functionally critical sites while achieving scalability, and in its direct validation pathway against emerging total scattering data for proteins in crystals, a method Dr. Case's group has recently pioneered.

proposals.title: A Fragment-Centric Algorithm for Perceiving Dynamical Motifs and Solvent Networks in Fragment-Based Drug Design
proposals.motivation: Fragment-based drug design (FBDD) relies on identifying small molecular fragments that bind weakly but specifically to protein targets. Current computational methods for analyzing fragment binding often treat the protein as static or use global dynamics analyses, failing to capture the localized, correlated motions and explicit solvent networks that are critical for fragment recognition and binding entropy. A specialized tool to perceive these fragment-scale dynamical and solvation 'types' is needed to improve the prediction and optimization of fragment hits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing an algorithm specifically tuned to perceive dynamical motifs and solvent structure at the scale of molecular fragments, we can more accurately classify fragment-binding environments and predict their binding affinities and growth vectors.
proposals.method: We will adapt and scale down the correlated motion analysis methods from our X-ray scattering and NMR work to operate on local regions defined by fragment-binding sites. The algorithm will integrate: 1) Local principal component analysis (PCA) of molecular dynamics trajectories centered on the fragment, 2) A graph-based network analysis to characterize explicit water and lipid (e.g., phosphatidylinositol) interactions using 3D-RISM or explicit solvent simulations, and 3) A machine learning classifier (e.g., random forest or shallow neural network) trained on known fragment-protein complexes to categorize fragment 'types' based on their dynamical and solvation signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new computational workflow for FBDD that explicitly accounts for local protein dynamics and solvent structure. It will deliver a publicly available tool for classifying fragment-binding modes and generate testable predictions about which fragment scaffolds are most amenable to optimization for specific, dynamic protein targets.
proposals.novelty: While protein dynamics analysis is common, specializing these methods for the specific scale and context of fragment binding—integrating correlated motion with a detailed, fragment-centric solvation model—is a novel and targeted approach not present in pre-2026 FBDD toolkits.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Extracellular Enzymes via Generalized Born Models to Program Soil Organic Matter Aggregation
proposals.motivation: Soil aggregation is a critical ecosystem process that regulates carbon sequestration, water retention, and fertility, yet it remains poorly predictable. While microbial extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) and enzymes are known to drive aggregation by interacting with organic matter (OM), the molecular determinants of these interactions are not understood. This gap prevents the rational engineering of soil microbiomes for enhanced ecosystem services.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the solvation free energy of key extracellular enzymes, computationally optimized for specific OM substrates (e.g., lignin, cellulose), is a primary determinant of their efficacy in directing microbial-driven soil aggregation. Engineered solvation properties will lead to predictable and enhanced aggregation outcomes.
proposals.method: We will apply inverse Generalized Born (GB) models, as developed in the Case lab for protein-solvent interactions, to design novel sequences for model extracellular enzymes (e.g., laccases, cellulases). The design objective will be to optimize the computed solvation free energy for binding target OM types. Designed sequences will be synthesized and expressed in model soil bacteria (e.g., Pseudomonas putida). We will quantify aggregation in controlled microcosms using wet-sieving and laser diffraction, correlating outcomes with enzyme surface properties and dynamics analyzed via molecular dynamics simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal link between computationally designed protein solvation properties and a complex soil ecosystem function. It will provide a novel computational-experimental pipeline for engineering soil microbiomes at the molecular level. The results will advance fundamental understanding of how protein dynamics and solvent interactions govern biogeochemical processes.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in fusing inverse GB-based computational protein design, typically used for folding and binding, with soil microbial ecology to solve an environmental engineering problem. It moves beyond correlative studies to actively design protein sequences to control a macroscopic soil property.

proposals.title: Coarse-Grained Molecular Dynamics of Fungal Supramolecular Assemblies: Predicting the Impact of Warming and Microplastic Stressors on Structural Stability and Function
proposals.motivation: Fungi are crucial for ecosystem health, but their molecular-scale responses to combined environmental stressors like warming and microplastics are poorly understood. Current models lack the ability to simulate the large-scale, multi-protein complexes—such as melanin-protein assemblies in cell walls or secretory enzyme clusters—that govern fungal stress resilience and function. A computational framework to model these supramolecular assemblies under stress is needed to move from phenomenological observations to mechanistic, predictive understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that warming and microplastic adsorption act synergistically to destabilize key fungal supramolecular assemblies by altering correlated protein motions and solvent dynamics, leading to predictable changes in assembly stability, aggregation propensity, and emergent enzymatic function.
proposals.method: We will adapt and parallelize the coarse-grained MARTINI force field to build models of fungal supramolecular assemblies, informed by available structural data (e.g., from X-ray scattering and cryo-EM). We will integrate explicit solvent and phosphatidylinositol lipid models where relevant. Long-timescale dynamics (microseconds) will be simulated using the OpenMM toolkit on GPU clusters, employing enhanced sampling techniques like replica exchange to probe stressor-induced changes. Analysis will focus on quantifying correlated motions (using methods from our prior work) and assembly stability metrics under varying temperature and surface-binding conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computational platform to simulate entire fungal supramolecular assemblies under environmental stress, linking atomic-scale interactions to mesoscale structural outcomes. It will yield testable predictions for how specific stressors alter fungal cell wall integrity and secretory function, offering a mechanistic bridge between molecular biophysics and environmental mycology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted application and scaling of coarse-grained MD to environmentally relevant fungal assemblies—a specific biological context largely unexplored computationally—and in directly coupling these simulations to the biophysical analysis of correlated motions under dual abiotic stressors.

proposals.title: Amino-Acid-Specific Side-Chain Torsional Potentials: Refining Force Fields to Capture Conformational Dynamics and Binding
proposals.motivation: Current molecular mechanics force fields (e.g., CHARMM, AMBER) treat side-chain torsional potentials as generic, independent of the local amino-acid backbone and sequence context. This simplification may limit the accurate description of correlated motions between side-chains and backbones, which are critical for protein function, allostery, and ligand binding. Given Dr. Case's recent work on pervasive dynamics and correlated motions, a systematic investigation into the validity of this assumption is a crucial next step for improving predictive modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: Side-chain torsional energy profiles are not universal but are modulated by the local chemical environment defined by the amino-acid type and backbone conformation, and incorporating this dependence will significantly improve the accuracy of simulated protein dynamics and conformational ensembles.
proposals.method: We will leverage high-quality, pre-2026 quantum mechanical (QM) calculations on dipeptide and tripeptide fragments in multiple backbone conformations (α-helix, β-sheet, coil) to derive amino-acid-specific side-chain torsion potentials (χ angles). These will be integrated into the CHARMM force field. The new parameters will be validated against experimental benchmarks, including NMR scalar couplings (3J) and order parameters (S²) for side-chain dynamics, and total X-ray scattering data from crystals to assess correlated motions, building directly on the group's established experimental workflows.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a next-generation, proof-of-concept force field with amino-acid-dependent side-chain torsion terms, directly testable against dynamical data. It will provide a foundational framework for more accurate simulations of protein conformational landscapes, with direct implications for understanding molecular recognition, allostery, and the design of targeted inhibitors, particularly for systems like phosphatidylinositol-binding proteins.
proposals.novelty: While backbone φ/ψ potentials are recognized as amino-acid-dependent, the explicit extension of this principle to all side-chain dihedrals is a radical departure from standard practice. This challenges a core, simplifying assumption of decades of force field development by proposing that side-chain energetics are intrinsically context-dependent.

proposals.title: Ensemble-Derived Electrostatic and Solvation Parameters for Improved Ligand-Binding Affinity Predictions
proposals.motivation: Computational drug design and molecular mechanics force fields rely heavily on parameters derived from static, high-resolution crystal structures. This neglects the pervasive dynamics and conformational heterogeneity of proteins, which are crucial for function and ligand recognition. Using static structures can introduce systematic errors in predicting binding affinities and modeling protein-ligand interactions, as evidenced by the Case group's work on correlated motions and experimental dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: That electrostatic and implicit solvation parameters (e.g., partial atomic charges, Born radii) derived from dynamic, ensemble-averaged structural data (NMR ensembles or molecular dynamics simulations) will yield more accurate and transferable models for predicting protein-ligand binding affinities compared to parameters derived from single static crystal structures.
proposals.method: We will generate conformational ensembles for a curated set of pharmaceutically relevant proteins (e.g., kinases, phosphatases) using long-timescale molecular dynamics simulations with the Amber force field and, where available, NMR ensemble data. From these ensembles, we will compute ensemble-averaged electrostatic potentials using the Poisson-Boltzmann method via APBS to derive dynamic partial charge sets. Similarly, we will calculate ensemble-averaged solvent-accessible surface areas to parameterize generalized Born (GB) models with dynamic Born radii. These dynamic parameters will be integrated into the Amber simulation package and tested for their ability to predict absolute binding free energies using alchemical free energy perturbation (FEP) calculations on known ligand sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, publicly available database of ensemble-derived parameters for key protein targets, directly linking protein dynamics to molecular mechanics energy functions. It will demonstrate a measurable improvement in the accuracy of binding affinity predictions in retrospective tests, offering a practical methodology to incorporate dynamics into structure-based drug design pipelines that currently rely on static structures.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble methods exist for refining structural models, the systematic derivation of fundamental molecular mechanics force field parameters from dynamic ensembles, rather than from a single minimized conformation, represents a significant conceptual and practical shift. This approach moves beyond using dynamics for sampling and explicitly encodes dynamic information into the energy function itself.

proposals.title: Beyond Two-State Validation: Assessing the Transferability of Force Fields from Simple Peptide Folding to Complex Multi-State Protein Conformational Transitions
proposals.motivation: Molecular dynamics force fields are often validated using the folding of simple, two-state peptides like Trp-cage or villin headpiece. While successful folding of these systems is a necessary benchmark, it remains unclear if this success guarantees accuracy for more complex, functionally relevant protein dynamics, such as conformational changes upon ligand binding or allosteric transitions. This is a critical gap, as force fields are widely used to study such complex biomolecular processes in drug discovery and enzymology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that force fields which accurately fold simple two-state peptides may still fail to correctly model the relative populations, kinetics, and correlated motions of intermediate states in complex, multi-state protein folding and binding pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ enhanced sampling methods, specifically temperature replica-exchange molecular dynamics (T-REMD) and Markov State Models (MSMs), to map the free energy landscapes of both canonical two-state peptides and proteins with complex folding/binding pathways (e.g., kinases, PDZ domains). We will use the Amber ff19SB force field with the OPC water model as a baseline. Analysis will focus on quantifying state populations, transition rates, and the degree of correlated motion (using mutual information and time-lagged independent component analysis) to identify systematic discrepancies between model systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative framework for the next generation of force field validation, moving beyond binary folding metrics. It will identify specific dynamic and thermodynamic deficiencies that are masked by simple peptide tests, guiding future force field development. The results will inform best practices for simulating complex biomolecular mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While two-state peptide folding is a standard test, explicitly challenging its sufficiency for validating simulations of multi-state conformational ensembles is novel. The approach directly links Case's expertise in correlated protein motions and X-ray scattering to a core methodological problem in computational biophysics.

proposals.title: Knowledge-Based Optimization of Nucleotide-Dependent Torsion Parameters for Improved Nucleic Acid Force Fields
proposals.motivation: Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of nucleic acids are critical for understanding processes like gene regulation and drug binding, but their accuracy is limited by force field parameters. Current additive force fields (e.g., AMBER parm99/parmbsc1, CHARMM36) use uniform torsion potentials for backbone and glycosidic dihedrals, which may not capture the nuanced, sequence-dependent conformational landscapes evident in high-resolution structural databases. This gap hinders the simulation of biologically relevant motions and ligand interactions in DNA and RNA.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deriving nucleotide- and sequence-specific torsion parameters through a knowledge-based optimization method, analogous to successful approaches for proteins, will yield a more accurate force field that better reproduces the conformational diversity and dynamics of nucleic acids observed in experimental structures.
proposals.method: We will apply the framework used to develop the AMBER ff99SB*-ILDN protein force field, which optimizes torsion potentials against a statistical potential derived from high-resolution protein structures. We will curate a large, non-redundant database of high-quality DNA and RNA crystal structures (e.g., from the Protein Data Bank). Using this database, we will compute residue- and sequence-dependent dihedral distributions for key torsions (e.g., α, β, γ, ε, ζ, χ). We will then iteratively optimize the relevant torsion parameters in the AMBER force field via a Monte Carlo/simplex algorithm to minimize the difference between MD-simulated and database-derived dihedral populations, using the `parmed` and `pmemd` tools.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, publicly available parameter set for nucleic acids that explicitly incorporates nucleotide-dependent conformational preferences. It will provide a more physically realistic model for simulating nucleic acid flexibility, folding, and macromolecular interactions, directly benefiting studies of riboswitches, protein-DNA recognition, and nucleic acid-targeted drug design. The project will also extend the philosophy of knowledge-based, dynamics-informed parameter optimization from the protein to the nucleic acid domain.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge-based methods have refined protein force fields, their systematic application to create nucleotide- and sequence-dependent nucleic acid torsion parameters is novel. This moves beyond the standard practice of using uniform torsions or ad-hoc corrections (e.g., parmbsc1) by directly embedding sequence-context information from structural bioinformatics into the energy function.

proposals.title: Refining AMBER Force Field Torsional Parameters Using a Large-Scale, High-Resolution Database of Protein Conformational Preferences
proposals.motivation: Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of proteins rely on accurate force fields, but parameterization, especially for backbone and sidechain torsional potentials, often depends on limited quantum mechanical data or small structural datasets. This can lead to inaccuracies in modeling conformational dynamics and protein-ligand interactions, as highlighted by Dr. Case's work on correlated motions. The rapid expansion of high-resolution protein structures in the PDB now provides an unprecedented opportunity for data-driven refinement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically optimizing torsional parameters in the AMBER force field against a large, curated database of high-resolution protein conformational preferences will yield a more accurate and transferable parameter set, leading to improved simulation of protein dynamics and binding.
proposals.method: We will curate a non-redundant database of ultra-high-resolution (<1.2 Å) protein structures from the PDB. Using the `ff19SB` AMBER force field as a baseline, we will employ a Bayesian optimization framework to iteratively adjust backbone (φ/ψ) and sidechain (χ) torsional parameters. The objective function will minimize the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the observed conformational distributions in the database and those generated by short, explicit-solvent MD simulations of model peptides and protein fragments. Validation will involve testing the refined parameters on independent protein systems and comparing simulated NMR order parameters and conformational exchange rates to experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a refined, publicly available AMBER parameter set (e.g., `ffXXSB`) with demonstrably improved accuracy for modeling protein conformational landscapes. It will establish a robust, scalable workflow for force field refinement that directly incorporates experimental structural statistics, bridging the gap between static structural databases and dynamic simulation models.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge-based potentials exist, this approach uniquely integrates large-scale structural data directly into the optimization of a physics-based, all-atom force field's fundamental parameters, moving beyond statistical potentials to improve the physical model's inherent accuracy for dynamics.

proposals.title: Learning Amino-Acid-Specific Torsional Potentials from MD Trajectories via Neural Networks for Improved Modeling of Protein Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Accurate molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of protein motions and binding depend critically on the underlying force field parameters. Current torsional parameters for protein backbones and sidechains are often derived from small molecule data or limited quantum mechanics calculations, which may not fully capture the complex, context-dependent energetics observed in folded proteins. This limitation hinders the simulation of functionally important correlated motions and conformational changes, a central theme of Dr. Case's recent work on experimental evidence for pervasive dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: Neural networks can learn amino-acid-specific torsion energy profiles directly from high-quality, long-timescale MD trajectories, and these learned potentials will improve the agreement of simulated dynamics with experimental observables such as NMR order parameters and X-ray scattering-derived correlated motions.
proposals.method: We will train deep neural networks (specifically, multi-layer perceptrons with residual connections) to predict the potential of mean force for key dihedral angles (e.g., phi, psi, chi1) as a function of local sequence and structural context, using features from extensive Amber ff19SB or CHARMM36m simulations as input. The training target will be the Boltzmann-inverted dihedral angle distributions from these reference trajectories. We will employ a transfer learning approach, fine-tuning networks pre-trained on general protein data with targeted data from simulations of specific systems like phosphatidylinositol-binding proteins. The optimized potentials will be integrated into the OpenMM simulation package for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, data-driven method for force field parameterization that directly links simulation data to improved torsional potentials. It will contribute a publicly available toolset for refining protein force fields and provide specific, optimized parameters for simulating dynamic processes like ligand binding and allostery. The results will advance the goal of achieving robust, experimentally-validated models of protein motion.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been used for force field development, applying it to learn sequence- and context-dependent torsion parameters directly from the ensemble statistics of protein MD trajectories, rather than from quantum mechanics on fragments, is a novel approach. It leverages the growing corpus of long-timescale simulation data to address known limitations in fixed torsional potentials.

proposals.title: Inverse Force Field Design: Deriving Sequence-Structure Compatibility from Amino-Acid-Dependent Parameters
proposals.motivation: Protein design traditionally relies on knowledge-based potentials and rotamer libraries, which may not fully capture the physical determinants of foldability and dynamics. The development of new, more accurate amino-acid-specific molecular mechanics force fields (e.g., CHARMM36m, AMBER ff19SB) provides a rigorous physical foundation for modeling. This project addresses the inverse problem: leveraging these detailed force fields to rationally design sequences that are thermodynamically stable in a target folded conformation, moving beyond static structural compatibility to incorporate dynamic stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequence can be computationally designed for a target protein backbone by optimizing a scoring function derived directly from an all-atom force field, and that such sequences will exhibit enhanced native-state stability and realistic dynamic profiles compared to those designed with statistical potentials.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline using the AMBER/CHARMM simulation ecosystem. The target scaffold will be parameterized using the latest amino-acid-dependent force fields (ff19SB, CHARMM36m). A Monte Carlo simulated annealing sequence search will be performed, where at each step, the energy of the folded state is evaluated via implicit solvent (GB/SA) energy minimization. A key innovation is the inclusion of a penalty term based on the variance of backbone dihedral angles from short, restrained molecular dynamics simulations to select for sequences that maintain the target fold under dynamic conditions. Clustering and analysis of designed sequences will be performed.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept, physics-based inverse design methodology directly linking force field parameters to sequence space. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating force field accuracy through the lens of design. The resulting sequences and methodology will provide testable predictions for experimental protein engineering and deepen understanding of sequence-encoded dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While force fields are standard for structure prediction and refinement, their direct use as the primary objective function for *de novo* sequence design is largely unexplored. This approach is novel in its explicit integration of dynamic stability assessments (informed by Case's work on correlated motions) into the sequence optimization loop, moving beyond static energy minimization.

proposals.title: Integrating the SMD Continuum Solvation Model into xTB for Enhanced Description of Protein Dynamics and Ligand Binding
proposals.motivation: Understanding the role of solvent in protein dynamics and ligand binding is crucial for accurate computational modeling. The semi-empirical quantum chemistry method xTB (GFN-xTB) is powerful for studying large biomolecular systems, but its default Generalized Born (GB) solvation model lacks the accuracy of more sophisticated quantum chemical implicit solvation models. Integrating a higher-fidelity solvation model would bridge the gap between computational efficiency and the physical accuracy needed to study correlated motions and binding events in proteins.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating the Solvation Model based on Density (SMD), a continuum solvation model parameterized from high-level quantum chemical data, into the xTB framework will significantly improve the description of protein conformational energetics, dynamics, and ligand binding affinities compared to the current GB implementation.
proposals.method: We will implement the SMD continuum solvation model into the xTB software package (xtb version 6.x). The implementation will involve coding the SMD non-electrostatic terms (cavity, dispersion, solvent structure) and interfacing them with xTB's electronic structure routines to compute the electrostatic component self-consistently. The modified code will be validated on small-molecule solvation free energies and applied to study the dynamics of model protein systems (e.g., BPTI, lysozyme) and the binding of phosphatidylinositol ligands, comparing results with explicit solvent simulations and available experimental data on correlated motions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a robust, open-source tool for efficient quantum-mechanical-based simulation of proteins in solvent. It will enable more accurate studies of protein dynamics, conformational changes, and ligand binding mechanisms at a semi-empirical QM level, directly supporting the interpretation of experimental data from X-ray scattering and NMR.
proposals.novelty: While SMD is established in *ab initio* QC, its integration into the semi-empirical xTB framework for large-scale biomolecular simulation is novel. This moves beyond simply using xTB's default GB model or applying SMD only to small molecules, creating a unique hybrid approach optimized for protein systems.

proposals.title: Mapping Rare Functional Motions in Lipid-Binding Proteins with xTB-Based Enhanced Sampling Molecular Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Understanding the rare, large-scale conformational transitions that govern protein function, such as lipid binding and membrane association, remains a major challenge. While enhanced sampling molecular dynamics (MD) methods are powerful for studying such rare events, their application is often limited by the computational cost of high-level quantum mechanical (QM) force fields, which are crucial for accurately modeling processes involving charge transfer or polarization, as in phosphatidylinositol recognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combining the semi-empirical quantum mechanical GFN-xTB method with modern enhanced sampling algorithms will enable efficient and accurate simulation of rare, functionally relevant conformational changes in proteins, revealing dynamics complementary to experimental X-ray scattering data.
proposals.method: We will implement and benchmark a workflow integrating the GFN2-xTB Hamiltonian with the OpenMM MD engine. Key enhanced sampling techniques, specifically Parallel Tempering metadynamics and Variationally Enhanced Sampling, will be adapted to work with xTB. This framework will be applied to study the conformational dynamics of lipid-binding domains (e.g., PH domains) in explicit solvent, focusing on rare transitions between apo and ligand-bound states. Simulation results will be directly validated against experimental total X-ray scattering data on protein motions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a practical, QM-informed enhanced sampling protocol for studying protein dynamics on microsecond-to-millisecond timescales. It will provide atomistic insight into the rare, large-amplitude motions that precede lipid binding, directly linking computational models to experimental scattering observables. The resulting open-source tools will bridge the gap between accurate QM descriptions and the long timescales required for functional biomolecular simulation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of a modern, efficient semi-empirical QM method (xTB) with state-of-the-art enhanced sampling algorithms for biomolecular simulation, a combination not yet fully explored pre-2026. This moves beyond standard classical force fields to capture electronic effects during rare events while maintaining computational tractability for sizable protein systems.

proposals.title: Functional Validation of Dynamically Coupled Proton-Transfer Residues in a Transport Protein via Mutagenesis and Kinetic Assays
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. Case's work on correlated motions in enzymes, have established that protein dynamics are essential for function, but directly linking specific dynamic networks to mechanistic steps remains challenging. For proton-coupled transporters, residues identified from molecular dynamics (MD) simulations as part of a correlated motion network are proposed to facilitate proton transfer, yet their functional necessity is often inferred rather than experimentally proven. This gap limits our mechanistic understanding of how dynamics enable catalysis and transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that residues Asp27 and Arg133, identified from previous MD analyses as forming a dynamically coupled pair, are essential for proton-coupled transport activity, and that disrupting this pair via mutagenesis will specifically decouple proton movement from substrate translocation without globally destabilizing the protein fold.
proposals.method: We will employ site-directed mutagenesis (e.g., D27A, R133A, D27A/R133A) on the target transporter. Function will be assessed using a radiolabeled substrate uptake assay in proteoliposomes under pH gradients to measure transport rates. Steady-state kinetics (Km, Vmax) and proton coupling stoichiometry will be determined. Global structure and stability will be verified via circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy and size-exclusion chromatography (SEC). MD simulations (using Amber or CHARMM) of the wild-type and mutant systems will be performed to analyze changes in the correlated motion networks and solvent accessibility, linking functional deficits to dynamic perturbations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct experimental validation for a computationally predicted dynamic functional network, strengthening the causal link between specific correlated motions and a fundamental biochemical mechanism. It will establish a rigorous combined computational/experimental framework for testing dynamic hypotheses in proteins, moving beyond correlation to demonstration of necessity.
proposals.novelty: While mutagenesis is standard, its targeted application to test a specific hypothesis about a *dynamically coupled pair* of residues—rather than a single active site residue—derived from advanced MD analyses of correlated motion is novel. This approach directly probes whether the dynamic coupling itself, not just chemical properties, is functionally critical.

proposals.title: Direct Determination of Functional Protonation States and Dynamics of Phosphatidylinositol-Binding Proteins in Lipid Bilayers via Solid-State NMR
proposals.motivation: The protonation states of key acidic and basic residues in membrane proteins are critical determinants of ligand binding, catalysis, and signal transduction, but they are notoriously difficult to measure in a native-like lipid environment. X-ray crystallography often provides ambiguous results due to low resolution or radiation damage, while solution NMR is not suitable for large, immobilized systems like integral membrane proteins in bilayers. A direct, high-resolution method to probe these states in situ is needed to understand the molecular basis of function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that solid-state NMR chemical shifts and dipolar coupling constants can be used to unambiguously determine the protonation states of specific histidine, aspartate, and glutamate residues within a phosphatidylinositol-binding protein domain reconstituted into a lipid bilayer, and that these states are modulated by lipid binding and correlated with protein dynamics.
proposals.method: We will express and purify a model protein domain known to bind phosphatidylinositol lipids (e.g., a PH or FYVE domain) and reconstitute it into oriented or magic-angle-spinning (MAS) lipid bilayers. Specific isotopic labeling (e.g., 13C, 15N at key residues) will be employed. We will acquire 2D and 3D ssNMR correlation spectra (e.g., NCACX, NCOCX) to obtain site-specific chemical shifts. For histidines, we will utilize 15N-1H dipolar coupling measurements from 1H-detected MAS experiments at high magnetic fields (≥ 800 MHz) to directly distinguish between the Nδ1-H and Nε2-H tautomers and the charged state. Dynamics will be probed via 15N relaxation and dipolar coupling order parameter measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust ssNMR workflow for determining protonation states in membrane-associated proteins, providing a crucial functional link between structural data and mechanistic models. It will deliver the first direct experimental evidence for the protonation microstates of key residues in a lipid-binding protein within a bilayer, and correlate these states with lipid-induced changes in protein dynamics, offering a new perspective on allosteric regulation.
proposals.novelty: While ssNMR has been used to study membrane protein structure, its systematic application to resolve functionally critical, ambiguous protonation states in a lipid bilayer environment—particularly for peripheral membrane proteins—is novel. Combining this with quantitative dynamics measurements directly links electrostatic states to the correlated motions central to Dr. Case's recent research themes.

proposals.title: Computational Design of Conformational Inhibitors Targeting State-Specific Dynamics in Transport Proteins
proposals.motivation: Many therapeutic targets, such as transporters and GPCRs, function by transitioning between distinct conformational states (e.g., inward- and outward-facing). Traditional structure-based drug design often targets a single static structure, which can lead to poor selectivity and off-target effects. Exploiting the full conformational landscape to design inhibitors that specifically stabilize a non-functional state represents a powerful but underexplored strategy for achieving high specificity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating multiple experimental conformational snapshots with molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, we can computationally design small molecules that bind with high affinity and selectivity to a specific, therapeutically relevant conformational state, thereby acting as potent conformational inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will employ an integrative computational pipeline. First, we will use long-timescale MD simulations, initiated from available inward- and outward-facing crystal structures, to characterize the conformational ensemble and identify state-specific cryptic pockets. We will then use the RosettaDesign suite, specifically the RosettaLigand protocol, to perform de novo small molecule design and docking into these state-specific pockets. The design process will be constrained by the conformational dynamics observed in the MD trajectories and will be iteratively refined using free energy perturbation (FEP) calculations to predict binding affinities for each state.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational framework for the rational design of conformational inhibitors, moving beyond static structure targeting. It will provide testable, high-specificity inhibitor candidates for a model transport protein and establish a generalizable methodology applicable to other dynamic therapeutic targets. The results will directly link fundamental protein dynamics research to practical drug discovery.
proposals.novelty: While state-specific docking is known, the novelty lies in the *de novo design* of novel chemical entities explicitly optimized to recognize and stabilize a single conformational state identified from dynamic ensembles, rather than screening existing compounds against a static structure.

proposals.title: Integrating Correlated Motions and Solvent Dynamics into a Next-Generation Force Field for Complex Carbohydrates
