proposals.motivation: Current molecular mechanics force fields (e.g., CHARMM, GLYCAM) for carbohydrates, while useful, often fail to accurately capture the complex conformational dynamics, anomeric equilibria, and solvent interactions critical for glycan recognition and function. This limitation hinders the simulation of biologically crucial processes like glycan-protein binding, where dynamics and solvent-mediated effects are paramount, as evidenced by pre-2026 studies on protein motions and phosphatidylinositol signaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carbohydrate force field parameterized against high-fidelity experimental data on correlated motions and total X-ray scattering, which reports on ensemble dynamics, will yield significantly improved accuracy in modeling carbohydrate conformational landscapes and their interactions with proteins and solvent.
proposals.method: We will leverage Dr. Case's expertise in correlated motions and robust X-ray scattering workflows. First, we will curate a benchmark set of oligosaccharide crystal structures and experimental NMR data on dynamics. Next, we will employ the CHARMM additive force field framework as a base, using quantum mechanical (QM) calculations (at the MP2/cc-pVTZ level) to refine torsional parameters for glycosidic linkages and exocyclic groups. Crucially, we will iteratively refine these parameters by targeting not just static structures but also ensemble-averaged data from total X-ray scattering of crystalline carbohydrates and solution NMR relaxation, ensuring the force field reproduces correlated motions and solvent interactions present in the experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, rigorously validated force field parameter set for complex carbohydrates, directly integrated into the CHARMM/OpenMM ecosystem. It will provide the community with a tool to simulate glycans with unprecedented accuracy in dynamics, enabling deeper insights into glycan-driven molecular recognition, enzyme mechanism, and the role of solvent in carbohydrate biochemistry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of experimental data on correlated motions and ensemble properties from X-ray scattering and NMR into the force field parameterization process, moving beyond the standard paradigm of fitting primarily to QM energies and static crystal geometries.

proposals.title: Graph-Theoretic Analysis of Correlated Motions from Total X-ray Scattering to Identify Allosteric Residues in Enzymes
proposals.motivation: Understanding how correlated motions in proteins govern function, such as enzyme catalysis and ligand binding, remains a central challenge in structural biology. Pre-2026 work by Case and others, using methods like total X-ray scattering, has provided rich experimental data on these dynamics, but robust computational frameworks are needed to extract functionally critical residues from these complex motional patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying graph theory to networks of correlated atomic motions, derived from experimental scattering data and molecular dynamics simulations, will robustly identify key hub residues that mediate long-range communication and allostery, surpassing the predictive power of static structure analysis alone.
proposals.method: We will construct dynamic correlation networks from molecular dynamics trajectories of target enzymes (e.g., those utilizing phosphatidylinositol), where nodes represent residues and weighted edges represent the magnitude of correlated motion. We will validate and refine these networks using experimental correlated motion data from total X-ray scattering workflows. Key residues will be identified using graph centrality metrics (e.g., betweenness, eigenvector centrality) and community detection algorithms (e.g., Girvan-Newman) to find hubs and bottlenecks connecting dynamic modules.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, experimentally validated computational pipeline for pinpointing residues essential for biomolecular dynamics and allostery. It will directly bridge high-resolution dynamic data from scattering experiments with interpretable network models, offering new targets for mutagenesis and drug design aimed at modulating protein function.
proposals.novelty: While graph theory has been applied to protein structures, its direct integration with *experimentally derived* correlated motion data from total X-ray scattering to identify dynamic allosteric residues is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond analysis of static contact networks or simulation-only studies.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Basis of Catalytic Efficiency in a Multi-Enzyme Pathway: A Multi-Scale Simulation Study of Coupled Dynamics and Substrate Channeling
proposals.motivation: Multi-enzyme complexes, such as metabolons, enhance catalytic efficiency through substrate channeling and coordinated dynamics, but the mechanistic principles governing this efficiency remain poorly understood at the atomic level. Pre-2026 experimental advances, including time-resolved crystallography and total X-ray scattering, have revealed pervasive, correlated motions in single enzymes, yet these methods struggle to capture the full complexity of coupled reactions and transport in large, dynamic assemblies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the superior catalytic efficiency of a multi-enzyme pathway arises from precisely correlated structural fluctuations across enzyme interfaces that facilitate both substrate transfer (channeling) and the optimization of individual enzymatic reaction rates.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-scale simulation strategy centered on a well-characterized system like the purinosome or a fatty acid synthase module. All-atom molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using the Amber force field, with explicit solvent and membrane lipids (e.g., phosphatidylinositol) where present, will establish the baseline conformational ensemble and identify potential channeling pathways. Markov State Models (MSMs) will be constructed from extensive MD data to map the free energy landscape of functional motions. These dynamics will be integrated with Michaelis-Menten kinetics and reaction-diffusion master equations to model the full catalytic cycle, testing how observed correlated motions influence overall pathway flux.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first atomically detailed, dynamical model of a complete enzymatic pathway, directly linking nanosecond-scale protein motions to microsecond-scale kinetic outcomes. It will establish a computational framework to quantify how structural dynamics contribute to metabolic efficiency, offering testable predictions for mutational and time-resolved scattering studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating state-of-the-art methods for analyzing correlated motions (from Case's recent work) into a unified model that simulates not just one enzyme's dynamics, but the coupled reaction-diffusion process across an entire functional complex, a scale of complexity rarely attempted pre-2026.

proposals.title: Optimizing Force Field Parameters Against NMR Ensemble Distributions to Capture Intrinsic Protein Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Molecular mechanics force fields are predominantly optimized against static, high-resolution X-ray crystal structures, which can bias parameters toward a single conformational state and neglect the intrinsic conformational heterogeneity of proteins in solution. This reliance on static structural databases, such as the PDB, limits the accuracy of simulations in capturing functionally relevant dynamics, including allosteric communication and ligand binding pathways. Integrating experimental data that explicitly encodes conformational distributions is a critical, unmet need for next-generation force field development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimizing molecular mechanics force field parameters (e.g., torsional dihedrals, nonbonded terms) directly against conformational ensembles from NMR spectroscopy will yield parameters that more accurately and robustly reproduce the intrinsic dynamics and free energy landscapes of proteins in solution.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline that uses Bayesian/Maximum Entropy refinement to optimize AMBER force field parameters against target distributions of backbone and sidechain dihedral angles derived from high-quality NMR ensembles (e.g., from the PDB). The optimization will target not just average values but the full probability distributions, using a Kullback-Leibler divergence metric as the objective function. The refined parameters will be validated by running extensive explicit-solvent molecular dynamics simulations on a diverse test set of proteins and comparing the resulting conformational ensembles to independent NMR data, total X-ray scattering profiles, and experimental order parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, dynamically informed parameter set for the AMBER force field, publicly released for the community. It will establish a generalizable framework for integrating ensemble-based experimental data into force field optimization, moving beyond the single-structure paradigm. The resulting force field is expected to improve the simulation of conformational transitions, cryptic site dynamics, and entropy contributions to binding.
proposals.novelty: While past force field optimizations have used NMR data as supplementary restraints, this approach makes the full conformational distribution the primary optimization target, directly encoding dynamics into the energy function parameters. This shifts the paradigm from fitting a static energy minimum to fitting a dynamic free energy landscape.

proposals.title: A Systematic Benchmark of Knowledge-Based Potentials for Predicting Protein Dynamics and Ligand Binding
proposals.motivation: Knowledge-based statistical potentials, derived from protein structure databases, are widely used for tasks like protein structure prediction, refinement, and binding affinity estimation. However, their performance in capturing and predicting the correlated motions essential for protein function and ligand binding is poorly characterized. A rigorous, comparative benchmark is needed to guide researchers in selecting the most appropriate potential for modeling dynamic processes, a core theme in David Case's recent work on pervasive protein dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that knowledge-based potentials will show significant, task-dependent variation in their ability to predict experimental observables of protein dynamics and ligand binding, and that potentials incorporating explicit multi-body correlations or solvent effects will outperform simpler pairwise models.
proposals.method: We will implement a standardized computational workflow to evaluate established knowledge-based potentials (e.g., DFIRE, DOPE, RWplus, GOAP, and our in-house potentials). The benchmark will use curated datasets of protein conformational ensembles (from NMR and MD simulations) and experimental binding affinities. Performance will be assessed by correlating potential energy scores with experimental measures of conformational variance, correlated motion maps (from Case's X-ray scattering work), and binding free energies for phosphatidylinositol and other ligands. Statistical significance will be evaluated using bootstrapping and cross-validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, dynamics-focused benchmark of knowledge-based potentials, creating a clear decision framework for the computational biophysics community. It will directly inform the development of more accurate models for simulating protein function and drug binding by identifying the strengths and limitations of current methodologies.
proposals.novelty: While benchmarks exist for static structure prediction, a systematic comparison focused on dynamics and binding—leveraging modern conformational datasets and correlated motion metrics—is novel. The integration of total X-ray scattering data as a validation metric for ensemble-level predictions is a unique aspect of this proposal.

proposals.title: A Genomically-Informed, All-Atom Force Field for Proteome-Scale Molecular Dynamics Simulations
proposals.motivation: Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are crucial for understanding protein function, dynamics, and binding, but are limited to individual proteins or small complexes. A force field capable of simulating entire proteomes would revolutionize systems biology, enabling the study of proteome-wide dynamics, allostery, and emergent interactions in a native-like cellular context. This is a critical next step beyond current studies of correlated motions in single enzymes or crystals.
proposals.hypothesis: A comprehensive, amino-acid-dependent force field, parameterized using genome-informed chemical distributions and validated against experimental data for correlated motions, can accurately simulate the structural ensembles and dynamics of diverse proteins across an entire proteome.
proposals.method: We will extend the CHARMM36/AMBER force field framework by developing a scalable parameterization pipeline. This integrates genomic data to weight amino acid-specific torsional and non-bonded parameters based on proteomic frequency. Validation will leverage Dr. Case's expertise, using total X-ray scattering to assess ensemble accuracy in crystals and NMR relaxation data to validate solution dynamics and correlated motion rates across a curated set of proteins spanning diverse folds. Machine learning (e.g., gradient boosting) will assist in refining parameters against this multi-modal experimental dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first openly available force field explicitly designed for proteome-scale simulation, accompanied by a robust validation suite. It will provide a foundational tool for simulating cellular processes at atomic detail, bridging the gap between structural biophysics and systems biology. The force field will directly enable in silico studies of proteome-wide response to perturbations like ligand binding or post-translational modifications.
proposals.novelty: While force fields exist for specific proteins, a genomically-informed parameterization for simulating the full chemical diversity of a proteome is unprecedented. The novelty lies in scaling atomistic accuracy to the systems level by explicitly integrating genomic sequence statistics and multi-protein experimental validation into the force field development process.

proposals.title: A Generalized AMBER Force Field Parameterization Framework for Non-Canonical Amino Acids to Model Protein Dynamics and Binding
proposals.motivation: The incorporation of non-canonical amino acids (ncAAs) is a powerful strategy in protein engineering to create novel enzymes, therapeutics, and materials with enhanced properties. However, accurate computational design and dynamics studies of such proteins are hindered by the lack of reliable molecular mechanics force field parameters for the vast chemical space of ncAAs. Pre-2026, parameterization efforts are often ad-hoc, limiting predictive power for conformational dynamics and binding energetics central to function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, automated parameter determination framework, extending the robust protocols of the AMBER force field, can generate accurate parameters for diverse ncAAs, enabling the simulation of their dynamic behavior and interactions with canonical proteins and ligands.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing General AMBER Force Field (GAFF) parameterization workflow. For each target ncAA, we will perform quantum mechanical (QM) calculations at the HF/6-31G* level to derive electrostatic potential (ESP) charges and torsional profiles. Parameters for bonds, angles, and dihedrals will be optimized to reproduce QM-derived geometries and vibrational frequencies. The framework will be integrated with the ff19SB protein force field and the TIP3P water model. Validation will involve molecular dynamics simulations of ncAA-containing peptides and proteins, comparing computed NMR observables, conformational populations, and binding free energies (via MM/PBSA or thermodynamic integration) against available experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a publicly available, validated database of force field parameters for a foundational set of chemically diverse ncAAs. It will directly enable more reliable computational studies of engineered proteins, predicting how ncAAs influence conformational dynamics, stability, and molecular recognition. This bridges a critical gap between protein engineering experiments and atomic-level simulation.
proposals.novelty: While parameterization for individual ncAAs exists, this proposal creates a generalized, automated, and extensible framework specifically designed for integration with modern, dynamics-focused protein force fields like ff19SB, ensuring compatibility for studying correlated motions and binding events in engineered systems.

proposals.title: Systematic Validation of a Modified Generalized Born Model for Capturing Correlated Motions in Large, Multi-Domain Proteins and Complexes
proposals.motivation: Understanding the role of correlated motions in large macromolecular assemblies, such as multi-domain enzymes and membrane-associated complexes, is crucial for deciphering allosteric regulation and binding mechanisms. Pre-2026 implicit solvent models, like Generalized Born (GB), are computationally efficient for studying dynamics but are often parameterized and validated on smaller, single-domain proteins, raising questions about their transferability to larger, more heterogeneous systems where long-range electrostatic and solvation effects are complex.
proposals.hypothesis: The modified GB model, previously refined to better capture correlated motions in specific protein systems, will demonstrate robust and transferable accuracy when applied to a diverse set of large (>500 residue) proteins and macromolecular complexes, accurately reproducing key dynamic features observed in experiment and explicit solvent simulation.
proposals.method: We will select a benchmark set of 8-10 large proteins/complexes with available high-resolution structures and experimental dynamic data (e.g., from NMR relaxation, X-ray scattering, or DEER). For each system, we will perform extended molecular dynamics simulations using the AMBER simulation package, employing the existing modified GB model (likely GBneck2 or similar variant) and the latest pre-2026 force fields (e.g., ff19SB). We will quantify correlated motions using mutual information analysis and quasi-harmonic analysis, comparing results directly to available experimental data and to control explicit solvent simulations for a subset of systems to assess fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical, systematic assessment of the scope and limitations of efficient implicit solvent models for studying dynamics in large systems. It will deliver a validated computational protocol, enabling broader access to studying correlated motions in biologically significant assemblies that are currently prohibitive for explicit solvent simulations. The findings will guide future model development for integrative structural biology.
proposals.novelty: While GB models are widely used for dynamics, a focused, large-scale validation on heterogeneous, multi-domain systems specifically for correlated motion—a key functional metric—is lacking. This directly tests the model's physical transferability beyond the smaller training sets typical of pre-2026 parameterization efforts.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Role of Conformational Dynamics in pH-Dependent Protein Function via Integrated pK Prediction and Ensemble Modeling
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of pKa shifts for ionizable residues is crucial for understanding pH-dependent protein function, allostery, and ligand binding. Traditional Poisson-Boltzmann (PB) calculations on single static structures often fail to capture shifts induced by conformational dynamics, a limitation highlighted by experimental evidence for pervasive protein motions. A model that reconciles PB electrostatics with conformational ensembles could significantly improve predictive power for biologically relevant processes like enzyme catalysis and signal transduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating explicit conformational ensembles, derived from molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, with continuum electrostatics calculations will yield more accurate and robust predictions of pKa shifts associated with large-scale conformational changes than static-structure PB methods alone.
proposals.method: We will employ long-timescale molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using the Amber force field and explicit solvent to generate conformational ensembles for target proteins undergoing known conformational changes (e.g., adenylate kinase). For each ensemble member, we will compute pKa values using the PBEQ module in CHARMM, which implements a finite-difference Poisson-Boltzmann solver. The final predicted pKa for each residue will be a Boltzmann-weighted average across the ensemble, with weights determined by the simulation free energies. We will validate predictions against experimental NMR and thermodynamic data for pH-dependent transitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, validated framework for incorporating protein dynamics into pKa prediction, directly linking conformational ensembles to electrostatic properties. It will deliver new mechanistic insights into how collective motions modulate protonation states to control function in specific enzyme and signaling systems, such as those involving phosphatidylinositol recognition.
proposals.novelty: While PB methods and MD are mature, their tight integration for pKa prediction across conformational states is not routine. The novelty lies in systematically exploiting the agreement between the MD model and PB theory to dissect the dynamic, rather than purely structural, origins of pKa shifts, moving beyond the single-structure paradigm.

proposals.title: Integrating Continuum Solvent Models as a Guiding Potential for Enhanced Sampling of Protein Dynamics and Ligand Binding
proposals.motivation: Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of proteins and their binding events are computationally expensive and often inadequately sample functionally relevant conformational states. Traditional workflows use continuum solvent models like Poisson-Boltzmann (PB) or Generalized Born (GB) only in post-simulation analysis, calculating solvation energies from pre-sampled trajectories. This decoupling fails to leverage the efficiency of implicit solvent models to actively guide the sampling process towards solvent-influenced, biologically relevant states.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating an implicit solvent-derived solvation energy term directly into the MD guiding potential or biasing scheme, we can more efficiently and accurately sample protein conformational ensembles and ligand binding pathways that are critical for function.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid sampling framework within the Amber/OpenMM ecosystem. The Generalized Born (GB) model (e.g., GB-OBC2) will be implemented not just as a post-analysis tool, but as a component of a time-dependent biasing potential. Specifically, we will use metadynamics or adaptive biasing force methods, where the collective variables are defined or reweighted based on the instantaneous continuum solvation energy of key protein regions or ligand poses. This will be tested on systems from Dr. Case's recent work, such as phosphatidylinositol-binding proteins, comparing sampling efficiency and correlated motions against traditional explicit-solvent MD and total X-ray scattering data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational protocol that directly couples efficient implicit solvent energetics with conformational sampling, potentially accelerating the discovery of rare protein states and binding intermediates. It will offer a new tool to bridge the gap between the detailed sampling of explicit solvent MD and the rapid energy evaluations of continuum models, directly addressing themes of protein motion and binding.
proposals.novelty: The inversion of the standard workflow—using continuum solvent to guide sampling rather than just analyze it—is a fundamental shift. While implicit solvent is used in some enhanced sampling methods, its explicit, dynamic use as a guiding variable for protein conformational sampling and binding, validated against experimental scattering data, is a novel integration.

proposals.title: Integrating Continuum Solvation Models with Molecular Dynamics to Quantify Lipid-Mediated Energetics and Dynamics in Membrane Protein Folding
proposals.motivation: Membrane proteins are critical for signaling and transport, but their folding and interactions are profoundly influenced by the heterogeneous lipid bilayer environment, which is computationally expensive to model atomistically. Pre-2026, combined molecular dynamics (MD) and continuum solvation methods, like the 3D-RISM and Generalized Born models, have successfully captured solvent effects on soluble protein dynamics and binding, but their rigorous application to membrane systems with explicit treatment of lipid headgroups and hydrophobic core remains underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid MD-continuum solvation framework, parameterized for phospholipid bilayers, can accurately and efficiently capture the thermodynamic forces and correlated motions governing membrane protein stability and ligand binding, providing insights comparable to fully atomistic simulations at a fraction of the cost.
proposals.method: We will extend the Amber/AmberTools MD suite, leveraging its existing Generalized Born (GB) and 3D-RISM implementations. The key innovation is the development of a heterogeneous dielectric/SAV (solvent accessible volume) model for a phosphatidylcholine bilayer, where region-specific parameters (e.g., low dielectric for hydrocarbon tails, gradient for headgroups) will be derived from all-atom MD benchmarks. We will apply this to study the folding of small helical membrane peptides (e.g., WALP) and the binding of phosphatidylinositol ligands to pleckstrin homology domains, analyzing energetics and correlated motions via time-lagged independent component analysis (tICA).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, efficient computational workflow for studying membrane protein energetics and dynamics, directly linking to Dr. Case's recent work on correlated motions in enzymes and total X-ray scattering. It will provide a quantitative, physics-based model for how specific lipid environments modulate protein function, bridging the gap between detailed atomistic simulations and coarse-grained models.
proposals.novelty: While continuum models are standard for soluble proteins, their systematic extension to asymmetric, anisotropic membrane environments with explicit parameterization for lipid chemistry is novel. This approach uniquely combines the efficiency of continuum solvation with the atomic detail of MD to probe lipid-mediated correlated motions, a dimension largely inaccessible to pure continuum or coarse-grained methods pre-2026.

proposals.title: Automated, High-Throughput Binding Free Energy Calculations with Explicit Solvent and Dynamic Protein Ensembles
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of protein-ligand binding free energies is a central challenge in computational biophysics and drug discovery. While rigorous alchemical free energy methods like thermodynamic integration (TI) and free energy perturbation (FEP) are available, their application remains labor-intensive, requiring expert setup and analysis for each ligand, which severely limits throughput. An automated, robust pipeline is needed to leverage these methods for screening large ligand libraries, a critical step for rational design.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating automated molecular dynamics (MD) setup, ensemble-based conformational sampling informed by experimental dynamics data, and rigorous error analysis, we can develop a robust computational pipeline that delivers accurate, high-throughput binding free energy predictions for congeneric ligand series.
proposals.method: We will develop a Python-based workflow using the OpenMM simulation engine and the pmx/alchemical-analysis toolkits. The pipeline will automate system preparation (protein parameterization with Amber ff14SB, ligand parameterization with GAFF2, explicit solvent box placement with TIP3P water and ions) and alchemical transformation setup. Crucially, it will incorporate initial conformational diversity by seeding simulations from multiple frames of existing MD trajectories or from experimental ensembles derived from the researcher's work on correlated protein motions and total X-ray scattering. Statistical analysis of convergence and uncertainty will be fully integrated into the workflow.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a validated, open-source software pipeline for high-throughput free energy calculations, significantly lowering the barrier to entry for non-experts. It will also deliver a systematic benchmark dataset on a pharmaceutically relevant target (e.g., a kinase or protease) to assess the accuracy and precision of automated predictions against experimental binding data.
proposals.novelty: The key innovation is the systematic integration of experimentally informed, dynamic protein conformational ensembles into an automated alchemical free energy workflow, moving beyond single, static crystal structures to account for protein flexibility and correlated motions in a high-throughput context.

proposals.title: Mapping Correlated Motions in the Ribosome: A Combined X-ray Scattering and Molecular Dynamics Validation
proposals.motivation: Understanding the functional dynamics of large ribonucleoprotein complexes like the ribosome is a central challenge in structural biology. While crystallography provides static snapshots, the correlated motions essential for tRNA translocation and peptide bond formation remain poorly characterized at an atomic level. Validating computational and experimental methods for capturing these motions in such large, asymmetric systems is a critical next step.
proposals.hypothesis: The combined methodology of total X-ray scattering from crystals and molecular dynamics simulations, previously applied to enzymes, can be robustly extended to the bacterial ribosome, revealing functionally important, long-range correlated motions that are conserved across functional states.
proposals.method: We will apply the established robust total X-ray scattering (T-XS) workflow to high-resolution crystal structures of the 70S ribosome in pre- and post-translocational states. These experimental diffuse scattering patterns will be directly compared to patterns calculated from multi-microsecond all-atom molecular dynamics simulations performed with the Amber force field, explicitly modeling solvent and ions. The comparison will validate the simulation's ability to reproduce correlated motion signatures, and subsequent quasi-harmonic and mutual information analyses will identify key correlated networks linking the decoding center, peptidyl transferase center, and GTPase-associated region.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, atomic-resolution model of correlated dynamics in a full ribosome, directly linking specific motion networks to mechanistic steps in translation. It will establish a generalizable benchmark for studying dynamics in mega-Dalton biomolecular complexes, bridging the gap between static structures and functional understanding.
proposals.novelty: While T-XS/MD integration has been shown for soluble proteins, its application to a complex of this size and asymmetry (ribosome) to extract and validate specific correlated motion pathways is unprecedented. The work moves beyond validating methods on single proteins to testing them on a fully functional, multi-component molecular machine.

proposals.title: Integrating NMR Residual Dipolar Couplings and Crystallographic B-factors to Refine and Validate Alchemical Free Energy Calculations of Protein-Ligand Binding
proposals.motivation: Alchemical free energy calculations are powerful for predicting protein-ligand binding affinities, but their accuracy is highly sensitive to the underlying conformational ensembles and force fields. Pre-2026, validation often relies on endpoint comparisons, lacking direct experimental constraints on the dynamic ensembles sampled during the calculation. This limits predictive reliability, especially for systems with significant conformational change or solvent-mediated effects.
proposals.hypothesis: That incorporating experimental data on protein dynamics—specifically NMR residual dipolar couplings (RDCs) and crystallographic anisotropic B-factors—as weak restraints during molecular dynamics (MD) simulations will generate more physically accurate conformational ensembles, leading to improved accuracy and reduced uncertainty in subsequent free energy perturbation (FEP) calculations of binding.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-path computational workflow. First, for target systems (e.g., kinases with phosphatidylinositol derivatives), we will perform extended MD simulations with the Amber ff19SB force field, applying time-averaged restraints to match experimental RDCs and ensemble-averaged restraints to match crystallographic B-factors, using the `pmemd` module. Second, these refined, experimentally-guided ensembles will serve as the baseline states for multi-state FEP calculations (using the `alchemical_analysis` framework) to compute relative binding free energies. The variance in free energy estimates across the restrained ensemble will be used to quantify dynamic contributions to binding entropy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, experimentally-grounded framework for assessing and improving the conformational sampling in FEP, directly addressing a key source of error. It will yield new insights into how correlated protein motions, detectable by total X-ray scattering and NMR, influence binding energetics for pharmaceutically relevant targets.
proposals.novelty: While experimental data guides structure refinement, its direct integration as dynamic restraints in the preparatory phases of alchemical free energy calculations is not standard. This approach uniquely leverages Dr. Case's expertise in correlated motions and robust scattering workflows to validate the simulation ensembles *before* the costly FEP stage.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Thermodynamic and Dynamic Role of Individual Water Molecules in Protein-Ligand Binding Using Correlated X-ray Scattering and Molecular Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Explicit water molecules are critical mediators of protein-ligand binding, influencing affinity and specificity, but their contributions are often obscured in ensemble-averaged measurements. Pre-2026, methods like crystallography identify static water positions, while NMR and MD simulations reveal dynamics, but a unified, experimentally validated framework for quantifying the energetic and dynamic impact of single waters is lacking. This gap limits our ability to rationally design ligands that exploit or displace key hydration sites.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual, structurally resolved water molecules at protein-ligand interfaces make distinct, quantifiable contributions to binding thermodynamics and correlated protein motions, which can be deconvoluted by combining high-resolution total X-ray scattering with advanced molecular dynamics.
proposals.method: We will employ a robust total X-ray scattering workflow on protein crystals, as developed in the PI's recent work, to obtain experimental pairwise distance distributions sensitive to correlated motions. This data will be used to validate and refine explicit-solvent molecular dynamics simulations performed with the Amber force field, focusing on systems with well-defined interfacial waters (e.g., kinases with phosphatidylinositol ligands). We will then use computational alchemy (e.g., free energy perturbation) to 'scale down' by computationally 'turning off' specific water molecules one at a time, precisely calculating their individual binding free energy contributions and analyzing the resulting changes in correlated protein motion networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, experimentally grounded map linking individual hydration sites to specific thermodynamic penalties/benefits and alterations in protein dynamical networks. It will deliver a generalizable methodology for assessing water molecule criticality in drug design and advance our fundamental understanding of how solvent orchestrates biomolecular recognition and dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of correlated motion data from X-ray scattering with atomic-level computational alchemy to isolate the role of single waters, moving beyond static hydration site analysis or ensemble-averaged solvation thermodynamics.

proposals.title: A Systematic Validation of Correlated Motion Analysis for Predicting Functional Dynamics Across Diverse Protein Families
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. Case's work, have established that correlated motions are fundamental to protein function, such as allostery and catalysis. However, computational methods for quantifying these motions from structure, like the updated server, are often validated on narrow, well-studied systems. A rigorous, broad-scale validation is needed to establish the method's predictive power for diverse biological questions, from enzyme mechanisms to membrane signaling.
proposals.hypothesis: The server's analysis of correlated motions, derived from molecular dynamics simulations and elastic network models, will yield robust, biochemically interpretable predictions of functional dynamics across structurally and mechanistically diverse protein families.
proposals.method: We will apply the updated server, which integrates total X-ray scattering constraints with molecular dynamics (MD) and normal mode analysis (NMA), to a curated set of 8-10 protein systems. These will include soluble enzymes (e.g., dihydrofolate reductase), allosteric proteins, and membrane-associated complexes involving phosphatidylinositol lipids. For each, we will compute correlation matrices and compare predictions against pre-2026 experimental benchmarks: NMR relaxation dispersion data, mutational studies affecting binding rates, and observed conformational changes upon ligand binding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical, publicly accessible benchmark for the community, demonstrating the scope and limitations of current correlated motion analysis. It will deliver specific insights into the dynamic mechanisms of the selected proteins and establish best practices for applying these computational tools to novel biological problems.
proposals.novelty: While individual methods exist, this systematic validation across a deliberately diverse protein set, directly linking computational predictions to multiple experimental functional readouts, is novel. It moves beyond proof-of-concept in model systems to a rigorous assessment of practical utility.

proposals.title: Predicting Mutation-Induced Changes in Protein Flexibility Using Total X-ray Scattering and Molecular Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Understanding how point mutations affect protein function is a central challenge in structural biology and drug design. While mutations often alter binding affinity by changing static structure, their impact on functionally important local dynamics and correlated motions remains poorly characterized and difficult to predict. A systematic, physics-based method to quantify mutation effects on flexibility would bridge a critical gap between sequence variation, conformational dynamics, and biological activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that point mutations systematically alter the local vibrational entropy and pattern of correlated motions in a protein, and that these changes can be quantitatively predicted by integrating molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with experimental total X-ray scattering (TXS) data from protein crystals.
proposals.method: We will apply the robust TXS workflow developed by the PI to obtain experimental ensemble-averaged pair distribution functions (PDFs) for wild-type and a series of single-point mutant proteins (e.g., in a model enzyme like dihydrofolate reductase). All-atom molecular dynamics simulations in explicit solvent, using the Amber force field, will be performed for each variant. We will compute theoretical PDFs from the simulation ensembles and refine the simulations against the experimental TXS data using a maximum entropy/bayesian back-casting method to derive experimentally-constrained ensembles. Key metrics like local root-mean-square fluctuations, entropy estimates from quasi-harmonic analysis, and correlation matrices of atomic motions will be compared across variants.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a combined computational-experimental pipeline for quantifying mutation-induced changes in protein flexibility. It will provide a foundational dataset linking specific amino acid substitutions to measurable alterations in dynamic profiles. The validated approach will offer a new tool for interpreting the mechanistic effects of disease-associated mutations and for engineering proteins with desired dynamic properties.
proposals.novelty: While MD studies of mutants exist, the novelty lies in the systematic experimental validation and refinement of dynamic ensembles using TXS, moving beyond static structures to test predictions about mutation-driven changes in correlated motions and local entropy.

proposals.title: ProteomeFlexDB: A Comprehensive Database of Predicted Flexibility and Correlated Motions for the Protein Data Bank
proposals.motivation: Understanding protein flexibility and correlated motions is crucial for elucidating mechanisms of catalysis, allostery, and molecular recognition. While methods like molecular dynamics (MD) and elastic network models (ENMs) can predict these dynamics, their application has been limited to individual, well-studied systems. A systematic, large-scale analysis of flexibility across all experimentally determined protein structures is lacking, hindering the discovery of general dynamic principles and the interpretation of new structures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-throughput application of validated coarse-grained dynamics methods to the entire Protein Data Bank (PDB) will reveal conserved patterns of flexibility linked to protein fold, function, and ligand-binding sites, creating a resource that can predict dynamic features from static structure alone.
proposals.method: We will develop an automated pipeline to process all high-resolution structures in the PDB. For each structure, we will run multiple established dynamics prediction algorithms: the Anisotropic Network Model (ANM) and Gaussian Network Model (GNM) from the ProDy toolkit for global flexibility, and use the CONCOORD algorithm to sample conformational ensembles reflecting steric constraints. The pipeline will compute key metrics including per-residue Debye-Waller factors (B-factors), hinge points, and correlation matrices of residue motions. Results will be stored in a queryable database (ProteomeFlexDB) with a web interface for visualization and download.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available database of predicted flexibility and correlated motions for the entire structural proteome, serving as a foundational resource for the biophysics and structural biology communities. It will enable large-scale bioinformatic studies linking dynamics to evolution and function, and provide immediate, model-based dynamic annotations for any newly solved or deposited protein structure.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the individual dynamics methods, but in the systematic scale of the application and the integration of multiple complementary models (ENM and geometric simulation) into a unified, publicly accessible resource designed for broad utility rather than single-protein analysis.

ideas.title: Ultra-fast QM/MM-GB scoring for high-throughput quantum-aware virtual screening
ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts by developing a hybrid QM/MM-GB method where the ligand (or a key binding moiety) is treated with a minimal, linear-scaling quantum mechanical method, while the protein and bulk solvent are handled by an ultra-fast, parameterized Generalized Born (GB) model. The key innovation is a drastically simplified, pre-computed GB reaction field and coupling scheme that allows the QM region's electrostatic potential to be evaluated within the MM/GB environment at speeds suitable for scoring millions of poses. This creates a novel pipeline for virtual screening that goes beyond empirical scoring functions by directly and rapidly assessing the quantum-mechanically derived interaction energy of a ligand pose within a solvated protein binding site.

ideas.title: QM/MM-GB with Discrete, Mobile Ions for High-Fidelity Electrostatic Modeling
ideas.description: This research direction combines a QM/MM-GB embedding scheme with the explicit inclusion of discrete, mobile ions within the Generalized Born (GB) continuum solvent layer. The core QM region captures electronic polarization and chemical reactivity, while a shell of explicit ions and counterions, embedded in and interacting with the GB field, models specific ion effects and local electric fields with high fidelity. This hybrid explicit/implicit ion model around the QM zone allows for the direct study of how specific ions modulate reaction barriers, binding affinities, and spectroscopic properties in complex biomolecular or materials systems. The resulting method provides a uniquely balanced and physically detailed model for processes where ion identity and local concentration are critical, such as in enzyme active sites with metal cofactors or at electrochemical interfaces.

ideas.title: Simulating Fungal Stressor Response at the Supramolecular Assembly Scale
ideas.description: This research direction combines the environmental mycology of Idea A with the computational biophysics of Idea B. It aims to adapt and parallelize coarse-grained (GB) molecular dynamics models to simulate entire, large-scale fungal supramolecular assemblies—like melanin-protein complexes in cell walls or multi-enzyme secretory clusters—under stress conditions. This allows us to move beyond species-specific phenomenological responses to mechanistically predict how warming and microplastic interactions alter the stability, aggregation, and emergent function of these critical fungal structures. The synergy provides a first-principles computational platform to link molecular-scale stressor interactions to organism- and ecosystem-scale functional consequences.

ideas.description: This approach generates a spatially varying dielectric constant for a macromolecule's interior by directly linking it to the underlying local electronic structure. The dielectric value at each point is computed from a constitutive model that uses local quantum mechanical descriptors, such as the electron density or polarizability tensor, derived from linear-scaling DFT or machine-learned surrogate models. This creates a rigorous, first-principles dielectric map that is uniquely determined by the atomic coordinates and chemical composition, eliminating arbitrary parameterization. The resulting model provides a more predictive and transferable foundation for calculating electrostatic energies, pKa values, and binding affinities.

ideas.title: Adaptive Voronoi Tessellation for Non-Spherical Atomic Volumes in Grain Boundaries
ideas.description: This idea challenges the spherical atomic volume assumption in grain boundary (GB) modeling by drawing an analogy from computational biology's approach to modeling protein surfaces. In protein structure analysis, the complex shapes of amino acid residues are handled not by pre-defined ellipsoids but by algorithms like the Shrake-Rupley method, which uses a rolling spherical solvent probe to trace a continuous solvent-accessible surface over the van der Waals atomic spheres. This accurately captures concavities and protrusions. Adapted to GB research, we propose defining atomic volumes via an anisotropic Voronoi tessellation. The space in the GB region is partitioned by using a non-Euclidean distance metric—where the 'distance' from a point to an atom is calculated using a tensor that incorporates the atom's local anisotropic properties (e.g., derived from electron density or elastic constants). The volume belonging to an atom is all points for which this anisotropic distance to that atom is less than to any other. This method dynamically generates complex, non-spherical volume shapes directly from physical atomic properties, moving beyond static ellipsoidal assumptions to a more fundamental and adaptable description of atomic partitioning at interfaces.

ideas.title: Inverse design of microbial protein sequences to optimize organic matter-driven soil aggregation
ideas.description: This research fuses soil science with computational protein design by applying inverse Generalized Born (GB) models to a microbial community context. The goal is to computationally design protein sequences for key extracellular enzymes, optimizing their solvation free energies to interact with specific organic matter types (e.g., lignin, cellulose). These designed sequences are then synthesized and expressed in model soil microbes to test the hypothesis that engineered solvation properties can direct microbial activity to produce predictable and enhanced soil aggregation outcomes. This transforms the study of soil aggregation from an observational correlation into a predictive, sequence-to-function design problem.

ideas.title: Adaptive Multi-Scale Terrain Modeling for Hybrid QM/MM-GB Simulations
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Geographic Information Systems (GIS) for embedding high-resolution terrain within a broader model to the challenge of QM/MM embedding with a Generalized Born (GB) solvent. The analogous GIS solution uses adaptive mesh refinement and blending in a transition zone to seamlessly integrate regional detail into a continental-scale model. The adapted research idea proposes to develop a hybrid simulation method where the quantum mechanical region is not static but dynamically adjusted by an algorithm monitoring the molecular mechanics environment solvated by GB. A mathematically smoothed transition layer, inspired by terrain-blending techniques, will bridge this adaptive QM core with the larger MM-GB field. This approach aims to enhance accuracy at the boundary, maintain consistent solvation effects, and improve computational efficiency for processes like reactive events where the region of quantum interest evolves.

ideas.title: Hybrid FMM-ML Surrogate for Full-Cycle Generalized Born Acceleration
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid algorithm that overcomes the pre-factor bottleneck in fast multipole methods for Generalized Born models. It combines a standard FMM for the long-range electrostatic sum with a local, kernel-based machine learning surrogate that predicts updates to the geometry-dependent Born radii. The surrogate is trained and refined adaptively during a simulation, using strategically sampled exact near-field calculations to maintain accuracy. This approach accelerates the entire GB calculation cycle, not just the pairwise sum, leading to robust speedups across diverse biomolecular simulations.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Neural Network for Robust Born Radii Prediction via Learned Analytical Model Correction
ideas.description: This redesigned approach embeds a differentiable, approximate Generalized Born model within a neural network framework to provide a strong physical prior. The network is trained not to predict Born radii directly, but to output a correction term that refines the radii from the embedded analytical model, ensuring baseline physical plausibility. The training loss combines standard data-fitting error with a physics-informed term that penalizes violations of key constraints, such as the relationship between self-electrostatic energy and the Born radius. This hybrid model maintains high accuracy on known data while drastically improving transferability and robustness to unseen molecular structures by anchoring predictions in established physics.

ideas.title: Adaptive Dielectric Mapping for Polarizable Generalized Born Models
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to integrate atomic polarizability with the efficient Generalized Born (GB) implicit solvent model for more accurate electrostatic response. Drawing an analogy from computational electromagnetics—specifically radar cross-section modeling, where complex scatterers are approximated using adaptive dielectric profiles on coarse grids—we adapt this solution. The adapted idea is to develop an 'Adaptive Dielectric Mapping for Polarizable Generalized Born Models.' Instead of adding explicit polarizable sites to every solute atom, the atomic polarizabilities of the solute will be used to define a spatially varying, field-dependent dielectric profile within the GB continuum. This creates a coupled system: the solute polarizes, perturbs the local implicit solvent dielectric, and this modified environment feeds back into the solute's electrostatic potential. This approach aims to capture many-body polarization effects and more accurate dielectric boundaries at a computational cost closer to standard GB, rather than full explicit polarizable force fields.

ideas.title: Machine-Learned, Environment-Dependent Atomic Shape Tensors for Grain Boundary Modeling
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the arbitrariness of predefined non-spherical shapes by using a machine learning framework to predict quantum-mechanically consistent atomic volume shapes. A neural network is trained on a large dataset of DFT-calculated electron densities to output an anisotropic shape tensor for each atom based solely on its local chemical environment. These predicted shapes are then integrated into a modified geometric model for grain boundary energy and structure, providing a physics-based, transferable, and scalable method that captures directionality in atomic packing without manual parameterization.

ideas.title: Mechanistic drivers of fungal aggregation: linking phenotypic response to genetic and metabolic pathways under combined warming and microplastic stress
ideas.description: This research moves beyond cataloging species-specific responses to identify the conserved and divergent mechanisms that govern fungal aggregation under stress. It employs a tiered approach, first screening a diverse fungal library for aggregation phenotypes under controlled warming and microplastic treatments to map response traits onto phylogeny. Second, for key responder and non-responder species, it integrates transcriptomic and exometabolomic profiling to pinpoint candidate genes and metabolites associated with aggregation. Finally, it tests causality by manipulating these candidate pathways in genetically tractable fungi to confirm their role in mediating the stress response, building a predictive framework for fungal community dynamics.

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Theory to Model Microbial Diversity-Temperature Effects on Aggregate Stability
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core framework of Modern Portfolio Theory from financial economics to microbial ecology. The analogous problem is managing risk and return in an uncertain environment, solved by quantifying how diversification and asset interactions affect portfolio performance under stress. Here, microbial species are treated as assets, their contributions to aggregate formation as returns, and the variability of this function under temperature fluctuation as risk. The adapted research would involve constructing synthetic microbial communities of controlled diversity, subjecting them to elevated temperature regimes, and measuring aggregation dynamics. The goal is to develop a predictive model where the 'portfolio effect' of microbial diversity—considering species identity, functional traits, and interaction networks—is explicitly linked to the stability of the aggregation ecosystem function under thermal stress, moving beyond simple correlative studies to a mechanistic, interaction-focused understanding.

ideas.title: Applying a 'Gut Microbiome Prebiotic' Framework to Test Organic Matter Amendments for Engineered Soil Aggregation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by applying a methodological framework from human gut microbiome research. In that field, different prebiotic fibers are tested for their ability to selectively stimulate beneficial bacterial taxa and functional outputs like short-chain fatty acid production. Adapted to soil science, various organic matter (OM) types (e.g., biochar, compost, cover crop residues) are treated as analogous 'prebiotic' inputs. The research would systematically incubate a standard soil with each OM type and use tools like high-throughput sequencing and metabolomics to examine how each input influences microbial community structure, selectively enriching specific functional groups (e.g., fungi, actinobacteria), and driving the production of biochemical agents (e.g., glomalin, polysaccharides) that directly mediate soil aggregation. This approach moves beyond measuring general microbial activity to defining causal links between OM type, specific microbial responders, and the mechanistic pathways to aggregation.

ideas.title: Use microfluidic pore networks with chemical gradients to study biofilm-influenced soil aggregation
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from biomedical biofilm studies to soil science. In the analogous field, microfluidic devices with integrated chemical gradient generators are used to model tissue environments and study biofilm dynamics. The adapted idea employs lab-on-a-chip techniques to create microfluidic networks that simulate soil pore geometries. Crucially, it incorporates programmable gradient generators to establish controlled, spatially varying distributions of root-derived or microbial chemicals across the pore spaces. This system allows for the direct visualization and quantification of how these chemical gradients influence microbial behavior, EPS secretion, and the physical process of soil aggregate formation within controlled, realistic pore-scale confines, moving beyond static chemical conditions.

ideas.title: Graph-Theoretic Analysis of Microfluidic Soil Pore Networks for Solute Transport Prediction
ideas.description: This research direction integrates microfluidic soil-on-a-chip devices with graph theory to fundamentally advance the understanding of solute transport in porous media. The controlled, visualized pore networks fabricated in microfluidic chips serve as physical, experimentally validated templates for constructing graph representations, where nodes are pore bodies and edges are connecting throats. Graph algorithms, adapted from solvation network analysis, are then used to identify critical percolation pathways, bottlenecks, and dynamically changing clusters as soil aggregates form or dissolve in real-time. This synergy enables the translation of direct lab observations into predictive computational models that can forecast transport behavior for contaminants or nutrients in complex, evolving soil architectures.

ideas.title: Adaptive Dielectric Map via Tomographic Reconstruction
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a uniform dielectric constant inside a macromolecule by proposing to determine a spatially varying dielectric map through an inverse problem approach, analogous to medical or seismic tomography. In those fields, an interior property (density, velocity) is reconstructed from indirect measurements. Adapted here, the macromolecule's interior dielectric constant is treated as an unknown 3D field. A tomographic reconstruction algorithm would be employed, using a diverse set of electrostatic observables (e.g., from simulations or experiments) as input data, to solve for the dielectric map that best fits all the data, thereby moving beyond simple predefined models.

ideas.title: Adapting Panel Clustering from Acoustics for GB Surface Area Estimation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using analogical reasoning from computational acoustics/electromagnetics. The analogous problem is the expensive O(N²) calculation of surface interactions in boundary element methods for wave scattering, which is solved using panel clustering or the fast multipole method to approximate far-field interactions hierarchically. The adapted research idea applies this clustering philosophy to grain boundary (GB) surface area estimation. By grouping GB surface segments into a hierarchical tree of clusters, precise area calculations are performed only for nearby segments within the same cluster, while interactions between distant clusters are approximated using pre-computed area moments or other geometric descriptors. This hybrid exact-approximate approach can significantly accelerate surface area computation for massive polycrystalline datasets while controlling the error tolerance, enabling more efficient analysis of microstructural evolution.

ideas.title: Adapting Financial Contagion Models to Map Stress-Induced Microbial Aggregation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from financial network analysis, where models of contagion and systemic risk (like DebtRank) are used to map shock propagation in interbank networks. The analogous problem is understanding how a localized failure triggers system-wide collapse. We adapt this by constructing directed, weighted ecological networks for soil microbial communities based on inferred metabolic exchanges or co-occurrence patterns. An environmental stressor is introduced as an initial shock. Adapted contagion algorithms then simulate the propagation of this stress through the microbial interaction network. This allows us to identify which microbial 'hubs' or cooperative guilds are critical for driving the community toward a stress-resistant, aggregated state—effectively modeling aggregation as a network-level 'bailout' or stabilization response to the propagating disturbance, revealing key targets for management or engineering.

ideas.title: Field-Validated Boundary Element Methods for Granular Biofilm Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction develops and validates a new class of granular biofilm (GB) models that integrate boundary element method (BEM) techniques for precise, geometry-aware surface area calculations. Crucially, the core innovation is to deploy and test these advanced numerical models not in simplified lab conditions, but directly within complex, uncontrolled field environments (e.g., soil, wastewater streams, natural aquatic systems). This forces the BEM-adapted models to confront true environmental dynamics—variable flow, heterogeneous substrates, and competing microbiota—thereby revealing which numerical refinements yield meaningful predictive power versus which are artifacts of lab simplicity. The synergy produces a validated modeling pipeline where numerical sophistication is directly guided by ecological reality, ultimately enabling accurate prediction of GB interaction effects and ecosystem functions in real-world applications.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Gradient Analysis to Validate Lab Interactions in the Field
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ecological solution of gradient analysis to challenge the assumption that interactive effects observed in the lab reflect field dynamics. In ecology, to validate controlled studies, researchers deploy field sites along an environmental gradient. Adapted here, the research would identify a key gradient underlying the lab interaction (e.g., resource availability, stressor intensity) and establish a transect of field measurement points along it. By quantitatively tracking how the interactive effects change across this gradient of real-world conditions, the study can determine the robustness and predictive power of the lab model, effectively bridging controlled and uncontrolled environments.

ideas.title: Robust Surface Area Estimation for GB Models via Regularized Boundary Integral and Capacitance Computation
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea addresses the foundational incompatibility between noisy GB geometries and classical BEM by introducing a dedicated regularization and physical proxy pipeline. First, the raw GB model is processed to generate a watertight, manifold surface mesh suitable for high-order quadrature. A boundary integral solver is then employed not for direct area calculation, but to compute the electrostatic capacitance of the shaped conductor, a quantity intrinsically linked to surface area yet more stable numerically. Finally, the capacitance result is converted to an effective surface area using a predetermined scaling relationship, yielding a robust metric that is insensitive to the local discretization artifacts that plague direct methods.

ideas.title: Deconstructing the lab-field gap through environmental gradients and causal mediation
ideas.description: This research employs a gradient design, deploying identical experimental units across a spectrum of control, from lab mesocosms to field plots, to observe how interaction effects decay or transform. Concurrently, a dense sensor network continuously logs abiotic and biotic variables (e.g., temperature variance, predator presence, soil heterogeneity) at each level. Key hypothesized drivers are then experimentally manipulated within this gradient framework to perform causal mediation analysis, pinpointing the specific environmental factors responsible for the loss or modification of lab-observed effects.
